questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: In the density measurement of a cube, the mass and edge length are measured as <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>10.00</mn><mo>±</mo><mn>0.10</mn></mrow></mfenced><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> and <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>0.10</mn><mo>±</mo><mn>0.01</mn></mrow></mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>,</mo></math> respectively. The error in the measurement of density is:
questionIds.solution:
Density ρ=massvolume
∆ρρ=∆mm+∆VV
=∆mm+3∆aa ∵V=a3
=0.110+30.010.1
=0.01+30.1
=0.31

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: A particle executes simple harmonic motion and it is located at <math><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mi>b</mi></math> and <math><mi>c</mi></math> at time <math><msub><mrow><mi>t</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mn>2</mn><msub><mrow><mi>t</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mi> </mi><mn>3</mn><msub><mrow><mi>t</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub></math> respectively. The frequency of the oscillation is:
questionIds.solution:
a=Asin⁡ωt0...(1)
b=Asin⁡2ωt0...(2)
c=Asin⁡3ωt0...(3)

adding equation (1) and (3)

a+c=Asin⁡ωt0+sin⁡3ωt0=2Asin⁡2ωt0cos⁡ωt0...(4)

∵  sinC+sinD=  2sinC+D2cosC−D2

from equation (2) and (4)

a+cb=2cosωt0

ω= 1t0cos-1⁡a+c2b ⇒f=12πt0cos-1⁡a+c2b

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: On a hot summer night, the refractive index of air is the smallest near the ground and increases with a height from the ground. When a light beam is directed horizontally, the Huygens' principle leads us to conclude that as it travels, the light beam,
questionIds.solution: Every point on the horizontal ray of light behaves as a source of light. Thus, the secondary rays which are directed towards denser medium would bend towards the normal. These phenomena cause the bending of the rays upwards.

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q:
Identify the characteristics of an adiabatic process in a monoatomic gas.
(A) Internal energy is constant.
(B) Work done in the process is equal to the charge in internal energy.
(C) The product of temperature and volume is a constant.
(D) The product of pressure and volume is a constant.
(E) The work done to change the temperature from $\mathrm{T}_1$ to $\mathrm{T}_2$ is proportional to $\left(\mathrm{T}_2-\mathrm{T}_1\right)$
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
questionIds.solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{Q}=\Delta \mathrm{U}+\mathrm{W}=0 \Rightarrow-\Delta \mathrm{U}=\mathrm{W} \\ & \mathrm{WD}=-\mathrm{nC}_{\mathrm{v}} \Delta \mathrm{T} \Rightarrow|\mathrm{WD}|=\mathrm{nC}_{\mathrm{v}} \Delta \mathrm{T} \propto \mathrm{T}_2-\mathrm{T}_1\end{aligned}$
$\therefore \mathrm{B} \& \mathrm{E}$ [Only possibility]

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q:
This question has Statement 1 and Statement 2 . Of the four choices given after the Statements, choose the one that best describes the two Statements.
Statement 1: If you push on a cart being pulled by a horse so that it does not move, the cart pushes you back with an equal and opposite force.
Statement 2: The cart does not move because the force described in statement 1 cancel each other.
options: Statement 1 is true, Statement 2 is true, Statement 2 is the correct explanation of Statement 1.
Statement 1 is false, Statement 2 is true.
Statement 1 is true, Statement 2 is false.
Statement 1 is true, Statement 2 is true, Statement 2 is not the correct explanation of Statement 1.
questionIds.solution: According to newton third law of motion i.e. every action is associated with equal and opposite reaction.

questionIds.solution:

questionIds.q: The frequency of a car horn encountered a change from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>400</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>500</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi></math>. questionIds.solution:
Wall as an observer
Frequency received by wall, 

f1=f0CC-V
Again wall as a source

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: The work done by a gas molecule in an isolated system is given by, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>W</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>α</mi><msup><mi>β</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><msup><mi>e</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mfrac><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mrow><mi>α</mi><mi>k</mi><mo> </mo><mi>T</mi></mrow></mfrac></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is the displacement, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>k</mi></math> is the Boltzmann constant and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi></math> is the temperature. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>β</mi></math> are constants. questionIds.solution:

⇒α=x2kT=L2ML2 T-2=M-1 T2

Now W=αβ2

β=ML2 T-2M-1 T2=M1 L1 T-2

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q:
The mean free path of a molecule of diameter $5 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{~m}$ at the temperature $41^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and pressure $1.38 \times 10^{5} \mathrm{~Pa}$, is given as $\_\_\_\_$ m.
(Given $k_{B}=1.38 \times 10^{-23} \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{K}$).
questionIds.solution:
Mean free path is calculated using $\lambda = \frac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2} \pi d^2 P}$
Given: $d = 5 \times 10^{-10}$ m, $T = 41°C = 314$ K, $P = 1.38 \times 10^5$ Pa, $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K
$\lambda = \frac{1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 314}{\sqrt{2} \times \pi \times (5 \times 10^{-10})^2 \times 1.38 \times 10^5}$
$= \frac{1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 314}{\sqrt{2} \times \pi \times 25 \times 10^{-20} \times 1.38 \times 10^5}$
Numerator: $433.32 \times 10^{-23}$
Denominator: $1.414 \times 3.14159 \times 34.5 \times 10^{-15} = 153.18 \times 10^{-15}$
$\lambda = \frac{433.32 \times 10^{-23}}{153.18 \times 10^{-15}} = 2.83 \times 10^{-8}$ m = $2\sqrt{2} \times 10^{-8}$ m

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: The ratio of wavelengths of proton and deuteron accelerated by potential <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>V</mi><mi>p</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>V</mi><mi>d</mi></msub></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>:</mo><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt></math>. Then, the ratio of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>V</mi><mi>p</mi></msub></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>V</mi><mi>d</mi></msub></math> will be
questionIds.solution:
Kinetic energy gained by a charged particle accelerated by a potential V is qV

KE=qV

Using relation between momentum and kinetic energy

⇒p22 m=qV⇒p=2mqV

We know that momentum of charged particle is p=hλ.

Thus, λ=h2mqV

Now, λpλd=mdVdmpVp

⇒12=2VdVp⇒VpVd=4

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: The magnetic field at the centre of a current carrying circular loop of radius $R$ is $16 \mu \mathrm{~T}$. The magnetic field at a distance $x=\sqrt{3} R$ on its axis from the centre is $\_\_\_\_$ $\mu \mathrm{T}$.
questionIds.solution:
The magnetic field at the center of a circular loop is $B_{center} = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2R} = 16\mu$ T.
On the axis at distance $x$ from center: $B(x) = \frac{\mu_0 I R^2}{2(R^2 + x^2)^{3/2}}$.
At $x = \sqrt{3}R$: $B(\sqrt{3}R) = \frac{\mu_0 I R^2}{2(R^2 + 3R^2)^{3/2}} = \frac{\mu_0 I R^2}{2(4R^2)^{3/2}} = \frac{\mu_0 I}{16R}$.
The ratio: $\frac{B(\sqrt{3}R)}{B_{center}} = \frac{1/16R}{1/2R} = \frac{1}{8}$.
Therefore: $B(\sqrt{3}R) = \frac{1}{8} \times 16\mu = 2\mu$ T.

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: For a particle in uniform circular motion the acceleration $\vec{a}$ at a point $P(R, \theta)$ on the circle of radius $\mathrm{R}$ is (here $\theta$ is measured from the $x$-axis)
questionIds.solution: For a particle in uniform circular motion, $$ \begin{aligned} & \overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}=\frac{\mathrm{v}^2}{\mathrm{R}} \text { towards centre of circle } \\ & \therefore \quad \overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}=\frac{\mathrm{v}^2}{\mathrm{R}}(-\cos \theta \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\sin \theta \hat{\mathrm{j}}) \\ & \text { or } \quad \vec{a}=-\frac{v^2}{R} \cos \theta \hat{i}-\frac{v^2}{R} \sin \theta \hat{j} \\ & \end{aligned} $$

12Iω2=Un-Ut

20×42=12(08)m2

100=ω2

ω=10rad

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: Pressure of an ideal gas, contained in a closed vessel, is increased by $0.4 \%$ when heated by $1^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Its initial temperature must be :
questionIds.solution:
Isochoric process
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{P} \propto \mathrm{T} \\ & \frac{\Delta \mathrm{P}}{\mathrm{P}}=\frac{\Delta \mathrm{T}}{\mathrm{T}}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & \frac{0.4}{100}=\frac{1}{T} \\ & T=250 \mathrm{~K}\end{aligned}$

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q:
A mixture of carbon dioxide and oxygen has volume $8310$ cm$^3$, temperature $300$ K, pressure $100$ kPa and mass $13.2$ g. The number of moles of carbon dioxide and oxygen gases in the mixture respectively are _______.
(Assume both carbon dioxide and oxygen gases behave like ideal gases) $[R = 8.31$ J/mol.K$]$
questionIds.solution:
Using the ideal gas equation, the total number of moles of the mixture is given by:

$n = \dfrac{PV}{RT}$

Substituting the given values:
$P = 100 \text{ kPa} = 10^5 \text{ Pa}$
$V = 8310 \text{ cm}^3 = 8.31 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$
$T = 300 \text{ K}$
$R = 8.31 \text{ J/(mol}\cdot\text{K)}$

$n = \dfrac{10^5 \times 8.31 \times 10^{-3}}{8.31 \times 300} = \dfrac{100}{300} = \dfrac{1}{3} \text{ mol}$

Let $n_1$ and $n_2$ be the number of moles of $CO_2$ and $O_2$ respectively.

$n_1 + n_2 = \dfrac{1}{3}$

The total mass of the mixture is $13.2 \text{ g}$. Using the molar masses of $CO_2$ ($44 \text{ g/mol}$) and $O_2$ ($32 \text{ g/mol}$):

$44n_1 + 32n_2 = 13.2$

Substituting $n_2 = \dfrac{1}{3} - n_1$ into the mass equation:

$44n_1 + 32\left(\dfrac{1}{3} - n_1\right) = 13.2$

$12n_1 + \dfrac{32}{3} = 13.2$

$12n_1 = \dfrac{39.6 - 32}{3} = \dfrac{7.6}{3}$

$n_1 = \dfrac{7.6}{36} = \dfrac{19}{90} \approx 0.21 \text{ mol}$

The number of moles of $O_2$ is:

$n_2 = \dfrac{1}{3} - \dfrac{19}{90} = \dfrac{11}{90} \approx 0.12 \text{ mol}$

The number of moles of $CO_2$ and $O_2$ are $0.21$ and $0.12$ respectively.

Answer: $0.21$ and $0.12$

questionIds.subject: Physics

questionIds.solution: From the graph, it is clear that for the same value of load, elongation is maximum for wire $O A$. Hence $O A$ is the thinnest wire among the four wires.

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: Earth has mass 8 times and radius 2 times that of a planet. If the escape velocity from the earth is $11.2 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{s}$, the escape velocity in $\mathrm{km} / \mathrm{s}$ from the planet will be :
questionIds.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{V}_{\text {escape }}=\sqrt{\frac{2 \mathrm{GM}}{\mathrm{R}}} \\ & \frac{\left(\mathrm{V}_{\text {escape }}\right)_{\text {Planet }}}{\left(\mathrm{V}_{\text {escape }}\right)_{\text {Earth }}}=\sqrt{\left(\frac{\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{P}}}{\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{E}}}\right) \times\left(\frac{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{E}}}{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{P}}}\right)}=\frac{1}{2} \\ & \left(\mathrm{~V}_{\text {escape }}\right)_{\text {Planet }}=\frac{1}{2}\left(\mathrm{~V}_{\text {escape }}\right)_{\text {Earth }}=5.6 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{s}\end{aligned}$

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: The electric current in a circular coil of four turns produces a magnetic induction <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>32</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math> at its centre. The coil is unwound and is rewound into a circular coil of single turn, the magnetic induction at the centre of the coil by the same current will be :
questionIds.solution:
Magnetic field at the centre of the coil is given by, B=μ0i2R.

Now in the first case:

B=4μ0i2R

and for the second case:

B'=μ0i2R'.

As the length of the wire will remain same, therefore

2πR'=42πR⇒R'=4R

Hence, required ratio will be

B'B=R4R'=116

⇒B'=2 T

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: Two cylindrical vessels of equal cross sectional area of $2 \mathrm{~m}^2$ contain water up to height 10 m and 6 m , respectively. If the vessels are connected at their bottom then the work done by the force of gravity is : (Density of water is $10^3 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^3$ and $\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$)
questionIds.solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{U}_1=(\rho \mathrm{A} \times 10) \mathrm{g} \times 5+(\rho \mathrm{A} 6) \mathrm{g} \times 3 \\ & \mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{i}}=\rho \mathrm{Ag}(50+18) \\ & \mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{i}}=68 \rho \mathrm{Ag}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & U_f=(\rho A \times 16) g \times 4 \\ & =(\rho A g) \times 64\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & \omega=\Delta U=4 \times \rho A g \\ & =4 \times 1000 \times 2 \times 10=8 \times 10^4 \mathrm{~J}\end{aligned}$

questionIds.q: A block of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> mass moves with a uniform speed in a horizontal circular groove, with vertical side walls of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. questionIds.solution:
N=mω2R

Given m=0.2 kg,T=40 S,R=0.2 m

Put values in equation 1

N=9.859×10-4 N

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: One mole of diatomic ideal gas undergoes a cyclic process ABC as shown in figure. The process BC is adiabatic. The temperatures at A, B and C are 400 K, 800 K and 600 K respectively. Choose the correct statement :
options: The change in internal energy in whole cyclic process is 250 R
The change in internal energy in the process CA is 700 R.
The change in internal energy in the process AB is  - 350 R
The change in internal energy in the process BC is  - 500 R
questionIds.solution:
Δ U CA = nc v T A - T C = 1 × 5 2 R - 2 0 0 = - 5 0 0 R

Δ U AB = nc v T B - T A = 1 × 5 2 R × 4 0 0 = + 1 0 0 0 R

Δ U BC = nc v T C - T B = 1 × 5 2 R - 2 0 0 = - 5 0 0 R

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: A point $\mathrm{P}$ moves in counter-clockwise direction on a circular path as shown in the figure. The movement of ' $\mathrm{P}$ ' is such that it sweeps out a length $s=t^3+5$, where $s$ is in metres and $t$ is in seconds. The radius of the path is $20 \mathrm{~m}$. The acceleration of ' $\mathrm{P}$ ' when $t=2 \mathrm{~s}$ is nearly
questionIds.solution:
$$
S=t^3+5
$$
$\therefore \quad$ speed, $\mathrm{v}=\frac{\mathrm{ds}}{\mathrm{dt}}=3 \mathrm{t}^2$
and rate of change of speed $=\frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dt}}=6 \mathrm{t}$
$\therefore \quad$ tangential acceleration at $\mathrm{t}=2 \mathrm{~s}, \mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{t}}=6 \times 2=12 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$
at $t=2 \mathrm{~s}, \mathrm{v}=3(2)^2=12 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$
$\therefore \quad$ centripetal acceleration, $\quad a_c=\frac{v^2}{R}=\frac{144}{20} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$
$\therefore \quad$ net acceleration $=\sqrt{\mathrm{a}_t^2+\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{i}}^2}$ $\approx 14 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: In a series <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi><mi>R</mi></math> circuit <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>X</mi><mi>L</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mi>R</mi></math> and power factor of the circuit is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math>. When capacitor with capacitance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>X</mi><mi>L</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>X</mi><mi>C</mi></msub></math> is put in series, the power factor becomes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. The ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mfrac></math> is
questionIds.solution:
In case of L-R circuit

Z=XL2+R2 Therefore, power factor

P1=cosϕ=RZ

As XL=R ⇒Z=2R

⇒P1=R2R⇒P1=12

In case of  L-C-R circuit

Z=R2+XL-XC2

According to the question XL=XC ⇒Z=R  ⇒P2=cosϕ=RR=1

⇒P1P2=12

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: The total length of potentiometer wire AB is 50 cm in the arrangement as shown in figure. If $P$ is the point where the galvanometer shows zero reading then the length $A P$ is $\_\_\_\_$ cm.
questionIds.solution:
In this potentiometer, for zero galvanometer reading, the potential at P must equal the potential at the junction between the 6Ω and 4Ω resistors.
The voltage distribution across the resistors is $V_{junction} = E \times \frac{6}{6+4} = 0.6E$ from point A.
Since the potentiometer wire is uniform with length 50 cm, the potential distribution is also uniform.
Therefore, $\frac{AP}{AB} = \frac{6}{10}$, giving $AP = 50 \times 0.6 = 30$ cm.

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: Initially a satellite of 100 kg is in a circular orbit of radius $1.5 \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{E}}$. This satellite can be moved to a circular orbit of radius $3 R_{E}$ by supplying $\alpha \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~J}$ of energy. The value of $\alpha$ is $\_\_\_\_$. (Take Radius of Earth $R_{E}=6 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~m}$ and $\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}$)
questionIds.solution:
Total energy of satellite in orbit: $E = -\frac{GMm}{2r} = -\frac{gR_E^2 m}{2r}$
$E_1 = -\frac{gR_E^2 m}{2(1.5R_E)} = -\frac{gR_E m}{3}$
$E_2 = -\frac{gR_E^2 m}{2(3R_E)} = -\frac{gR_E m}{6}$
Energy supplied $= E_2 - E_1 = -\frac{gR_E m}{6} + \frac{gR_E m}{3} = \frac{gR_E m}{6}$
$= \frac{10 \times 6 \times 10^6 \times 100}{6} = 10^9$ J $= 1000 \times 10^6$ J
$\alpha = 1000$

questionIds.subject: Chemistry
questionIds.q:
Given below are two statements for catalytic properties of transition metals.
Statement I: First row transition metals which act as catalyst utilise their 3d electrons only for formation of bonds between reactant molecules and atoms on the surface of catalyst.
Statement II: There is increase in the concentration of reactants on the surface of catalyst which strengthens the bonds in reacting molecules.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:
options: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
Both Statement I and Statement II are incorrect
Statement I is correct but Statement II is incorrect
Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is correct
questionIds.solution:
Statement I is incorrect because first row transition metals utilise both 3d and 4s electrons for the formation of bonds between reactant molecules and atoms on the surface of the catalyst.

Statement II is incorrect because the increase in the concentration of reactants on the surface of the catalyst weakens the bonds in the reacting molecules, which lowers the activation energy.

Therefore, both Statement I and Statement II are incorrect.

Answer: Both Statement I and Statement II are incorrect

questionIds.subject: Chemistry
questionIds.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questionIds.q:
Which of the following statements are not correct?

A. The electron gain enthalpy of F is more negative than that of Cl.

B. Ionization enthalpy decreases in a group of periodic table.

C. The electronegativity of an atom depends upon the atoms bonded to it.

D. Al2O3 and NO are examples of amphoteric oxides.

Choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
questionIds.solution:
A is incorrect. F and Cl belong to the same group of the periodic table. The electron gain enthalpy usually becomes less negative on moving down a group. However, in this case, the value of the electron gain enthalpy of Cl is more negative than that of F. This is because the atomic size of F is smaller than that of Cl. 

B is correct (ionisation energy decreases down the group).

C is incorrect (Electronegativity is a fixed property of an element and is typically measured on scales such as the Pauling scale or the Mulliken scale.). 

D is incorrect. The oxides which behave as both acidic and basic oxides are called amphoteric oxides. . Here Al2O3 is amphoteric oxide and NO is neutral oxide.

Hence, A, C and D are incorrect.

questionIds.subject: Chemistry
questionIds.q: Which of the following pictorial diagram most correctly represents the $\pi^*$ ($\pi$-antibonding) molecular orbital between two atoms if the internuclear axis is taken to be in the z-direction ($\xrightarrow{\text{z-axis}}$)?
questionIds.solution:
To determine the correct pictorial representation of a $\pi^{*}$ ($\pi$-antibonding) molecular orbital, we need to consider how it is formed.

1. A $\pi$ molecular orbital is formed by the sideways (lateral) overlap of atomic orbitals (like p-orbitals) whose axes are perpendicular to the internuclear axis (z-axis).

2. An antibonding molecular orbital ($\pi^{*}$) is formed by the out-of-phase (subtractive) overlap of these atomic orbitals. This means the positive lobe of one orbital overlaps with the negative lobe of the other.

3. Visually, this out-of-phase overlap results in a nodal plane between the two nuclei, perpendicular to the internuclear axis, where the electron density is zero. The lobes of the orbitals are often depicted as bending away from each other due to repulsion.

Evaluating the given options:
- Option (A) shows continuous electron density above and below the internuclear axis, which represents a $\pi$ bonding molecular orbital.
- Option (B) shows two p-orbitals with in-phase alignment (shaded lobe next to shaded lobe), which would combine to form a $\pi$ bonding orbital.
- Option (C) shows two p-orbitals with out-of-phase alignment (shaded lobe next to unshaded lobe). This creates a nodal plane between the nuclei, correctly representing a $\pi^{*}$ antibonding molecular orbital.
- Option (D) shows overlap along the internuclear axis with a node between the nuclei, which represents a $\sigma^{*}$ antibonding molecular orbital.

Therefore, the diagram in (C) most correctly represents the $\pi^{*}$ molecular orbital.

Answer:

questionIds.subject: Chemistry
questionIds.q: The correct order of reactivity of the following benzyl halides towards reaction with KCN is :
questionIds.solution:
Reactivity in SN2 reactions with KCN depends on the stability and nucleophilicity of the transition state.
For benzyl halides, substituents on the benzene ring affect the reactivity of the benzylic carbon through resonance and inductive effects.
Para-nitro group (-NO2) is strongly electron-withdrawing, activating the benzylic carbon toward nucleophilic substitution.
Para-amino (-NH2) is electron-donating, deactivating.
Para-hydroxyl (-OH) is weakly donating.
Meta-nitro group has reduced electron-withdrawal compared to para-position.
Order of reactivity based on electronic effects: para-NO2 (c) > meta-NO2 (d) > para-OH (a) > para-NH2 (b).
This gives b > a > d > c

options: Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is false but R is true
Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
questionIds.solution:
For line II ∆S is more negative than line I. In the Ellingham diagram, a sharp change in slope of the line is observed for Mg-MgO at ~1120oC because that is the boiling point of magnesium.

From diagram, ∆SI=(Ssolid)-(Ssolid+Sgas)∆SII=(Sliquid)-(Ssolid+Sgas)

There is a large increase in entropy associated with the change of state of magnesium.

So, both Assertion (A) and Reason (R) are true and (R) is the correct explanation of (A).

questionIds.subject: Chemistry
questionIds.q:
Regarding the hydrides of group 15 elements $\mathrm{EH}_{3}(\mathrm{E}=\mathrm{N}, \mathrm{P}, \mathrm{As}, \mathrm{Sb})$, select the correct statement from the following:
A. The stability of hydrides decreases down the group.
B. The basicity of hydrides decreases down the group.
C. The reducing character increases down the group.
D. The boiling point increases down the group.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
questionIds.solution:
For group 15 hydrides $EH_3$ (N, P, As, Sb):
Statement A is correct—stability decreases down the group as H-E bond strength decreases.
Statement B is correct—basicity decreases as the central atom becomes less electronegative, making it less willing to share electron pairs.
Statement C is correct—reducing character increases down the group as the hydrides become easier to oxidize.
Statement D is incorrect—boiling points do not consistently increase; NH₃ is anomalously high (-33°C) due to strong hydrogen bonding, but PH₃ (-87°C) is lower, and the trend is irregular.

Therefore, A, B & C only are correct.

questionIds.subject: Chemistry
questionIds.q: The delicate balance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> is NOT disturbed by
questionIds.solution:
Plants use up carbon dioxide during photosynthesis and release oxygen into the atmosphere. This maintains the delicate balance between oxygen and carbon dioxide in the atmosphere.

The burning of fuels and cutting of trees is disturbing this balance in nature. As a result of such activities, the amount of carbon dioxide in the air is going up and the level of oxygen is coming down. Continuous and excessive use of fuels and deforestation would increase this imbalance. This is bringing about a warming of the atmosphere, called global warming. Respiration, is a natural process, So balance of CO2 and O2 not disturbed by respiration.

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q:
Let $f(x)=\frac{x}{\sqrt{a^{2}+x^{2}}}-\frac{d-x}{\sqrt{b^{2}+(d-x)^{2}}}, x \in \mathbb{R}$ wherea, b

and d are non-zero real constants. Then :
options: $f^{\prime}$ is not a continuous function of $x$
$f$ is neither increasing nor decreasing function of $x$
questionIds.solution:
$f(x)=\frac{x}{\sqrt{a^{2}+x^{2}}}-\frac{(d-x)}{\sqrt{b^{2}+(d-x)^{2}}}$

$=\frac{x}{\sqrt{a^{2}+x^{2}}}+\frac{(x-d)}{\sqrt{b^{2}+(x-d)^{2}}}$

$\begin{array}{l}

f^{\prime}(x)=\frac{\sqrt{a^{2}+x^{2}}-\frac{x(2 x)}{2 \sqrt{a^{2}+x^{2}}}}{\left(a^{2}+x^{2}\right)} \\

=\frac{a^{2}+x^{2}-x^{2}}{\left(a^{2}+x^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}+\frac{b^{2}+(x-d)^{2}-(x-d)^{2}}{\left(b^{2}+(x-d)^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}} \\

+\frac{\sqrt{b^{2}+(x-d)^{2}}-\frac{(x-d) 2(x-d)}{2 \sqrt{b^{2}+(x-d)^{2}}}}{\left(a^{2}+(x-d)^{2}\right)} \\

\Rightarrow f^{\prime}(x)>0, \square x \in R

\end{array}$

$\Rightarrow f(x)$ is increasing function.

Hence, $f(x)$ is increasing function

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: A man on the top of a vertical tower observes a car moving at a uniform speed towards the tower on a horizontal road. If it takes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>18</mn><mo> </mo><mi>min</mi></math> for the angle of depression of the car to change from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>45</mn><mo>°</mo></math>, then the time taken (in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>min</mi></math>) by the car to reach the foot of the tower is
questionIds.solution:
Let length of tower be h.

⇒AC′=AB=h

And AC=ABcot30°=3h

⇒CC′=3-1h

Time taken by car form C C'=18 min

⇒Time take by car to reach the foot of the tower

=183-1 min

=93+1 min

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><munder><mi>lim</mi><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>→</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></munder><mfenced><mfrac><mrow><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>3</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>-</mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>3</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>7</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mi>a</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>b</mi></mrow></mfrac></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></math>, then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>a</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>b</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is equal to
questionIds.solution:
Given limx→1sin3x2−4x+1−x2+12x3−7x2+ax+b=−2    ...i

Putting x=1 in limit we get

⇒limx→1sin0-1+12-7+a+b=-2 ⇒limx→10a+b-5=-2

For limit to exist a+b-5 should be zero

so a+b-5=0 ⇒  a+b=5      ...ii

Now Using L-Hospital in equation i we get

limx→1cos3x2-4x+16x-4-2x6x2-14x+a=-2

Again putting x=1 we get

⇒limx→1cos0×6-4-26-14+a=-2

⇒limx→10a-8=-2

Again for limit to exist a-8=0⇒a=8

So from equation ii we get

8+b=5⇒b=-3

So a-b=8--3=11

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: If $\hat{x}, \hat{y}$ and $\hat{z}$ are three unit vectors in threedimensional space, then the minimum value of $|\hat{x}+\hat{y}|^2+|\hat{y}+\hat{z}|^2+|\hat{z}+\hat{x}|^2$
questionIds.solution:
$(\hat{x}+\hat{y}+\hat{z})^2 \geq 0$
$$
\begin{aligned}
&\Rightarrow 3+2 \Sigma \hat{x} \cdot \hat{y} \geq 0 \\
&\Rightarrow 2 \Sigma \hat{x} \cdot \hat{y} \geq-3
\end{aligned}
$$
Now, $|\hat{x}+\hat{y}|^2+|\hat{y}+\hat{z}|^2+|\hat{z}+\hat{x}|^2$ $=6+2 \sum \hat{x} . \hat{y} \geq 6+(-3)$
$$
\Rightarrow|\hat{x}+\hat{y}|^2+|\hat{y}+\hat{z}|^2+|\hat{z}+\hat{x}|^2 \geq 3
$$

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q:
Let $f:[0, \infty) \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ be differentiable function such that $f(\mathrm{x})=1-2 \mathrm{x}+\int_0^x e^{x-t} f(t) \mathrm{dt}$ for all $\mathrm{x} \in[0, \infty)$.
Then the area of the region bounded by $\mathrm{y}=f(\mathrm{x})$ and the coordinate axes is
questionIds.solution:
$\begin{aligned} & y=1-2 x+e^x \int_0^x e^{-t} f(t) d t \\ & \frac{d y}{d x}=-2+e^{-x} \cdot e^x f(x)+e^x \int_0^x e^{-t} f(t) d t \\ & \frac{d y}{d x}=-2+y+y+2 x-1 \\ & \frac{d y}{d x}-2 y=(2 x-3) \\ & y e^{-2 x}=\int(2 x-3) d x \cdot e^{-2 x} \\ & y e^{-2 x}=\frac{-(2 x-3)}{2} e^{-2 x}+\int e^{-2 x} d x \\ & y e^{-2 x}=\frac{-(2 x-3)}{2} e^{-2 x}-\frac{1}{2} e^{-2 x}+c \\ & f(0)=1 \Rightarrow c=1-\frac{3}{2}+\frac{1}{2}=0\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & y=-\frac{(2 x-3)}{2}-\frac{1}{2} \\ & y=-x+1 \\ & x+y=1 \\ & \text { area }=\frac{1}{2}(1)(1)=\frac{1}{2}\end{aligned}$

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: The complex number <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>z</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi>i</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mi>cos</mi><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>3</mn></mfrac><mo>+</mo><mi>i</mi><mi>sin</mi><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>3</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfrac></math> is equal to:
questionIds.solution:
Given,

z=i-1cosπ3+isinπ3

⇒z=2-12+i2cosπ3+isinπ3

Now argument of complex number -12+i2 is 3π4

So, by euler's formula eiθ=cosθ+isinθ we get,

⇒z=2ei3π/4eiπ/3

⇒z=2ei5π/12

⇒z=2cos5π12+isin5π12

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: Let $A=\left[\begin{array}{ll}3 & -4 \\ 1 & -1\end{array}\right]$ and $B$ be two matrices such that $A^{100}=100 B+I$. Then the sum of all the elements of $\mathrm{B}^{100}$ is $\_\_\_\_$
questionIds.solution:
$A = I + \begin{bmatrix} 2 & -4 \\ 1 & -2 \end{bmatrix}$, let $M = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & -4 \\ 1 & -2 \end{bmatrix}$
$M^2 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} = M^3 = M^4 = \ldots = M^{100}$
$A^{100} = (I + M)^{100} = \sum_{r=0}^{100} \binom{100}{r} M^r \cdot I$
$A^{100} = I + 100M = I + 100B$
$\therefore M = B \Rightarrow M^{100} = B^{100} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: Let $\alpha|x|=|y| \mathrm{e}^{x y-\beta}, \alpha, \beta \in \mathbf{N}$ be the solution of the differential equation $x \mathrm{~d} y-y \mathrm{~d} x+x y(x \mathrm{~d} y+y \mathrm{~d} x)=0$, $y(1)=2$. Then $\alpha+\beta$ is equal to ________
questionIds.solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& a|x|=|y| e^{y x-\beta}, a, b \in N \\
& x d y-y d x+x y(x d y+y d x)=0 \\
& \frac{d y}{y}-\frac{d x}{x}+(x d y+y d x)=0 \\
& \ell n|y|-\ell n|x|+x y=c \\
& y(1)=2 \\
& \ell n|2|-0+2=c \\
& c=2+\ell n 2 \\
& \ell n|y|-\ell n|x|+x y=2+\ell n 2 \\
& \ell n|x|=\ell n\left|\frac{y}{2}\right|-2+x y \\
& |x|=\left|\frac{y}{2}\right| e^{x y-2} \\
& 2|x|=|y| e^{x y-2} \\
& \alpha=2 \quad \beta=2 \quad \alpha+\beta=4
\end{aligned}$

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi></math> be a common tangent line to the curves <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>9</mn><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>36</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>y</mi></mrow></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>31</mn></math>. Then the square of the slope of the line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi></math> is ______.<gwmw style="display:none;"></gwmw>
questionIds.solution:
Given curves are x29+y24=1

x2+y2=314

let slope of common tangent be m

so tangents are y=mx±9m2+4

y=mx±3121+m2

hence 9m2+4=3141+m2

⇒36m2+16=31+31m2⇒m2=3

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: A spherical iron ball of <math><mn>10</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> radius is coated with a layer of ice of uniform thickness that melts at a rate of <math><mn>50</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi><mrow><mrow><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></mrow><mo>/</mo><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi></mrow></mrow></math> . When the thickness of ice is <math><mn>5</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> , then the rate (in <math><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mo>/</mo><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi></mrow></mrow></math> .) at which of the thickness of ice decreases, is:
questionIds.solution:
Let thickness =x cm
Total volume V=43π10+x3
dVdt=4π10+x2dxdt…..(i)
Given dVdt=50 cm3/min
At x=5 cm
50=4π10+52dxdt
dxdt=118π cm/min

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: The locus of the centres of the circles, which touch the circle, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math> externally, also touch the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi></math>-axis and lie in the first quadrant, is:
questionIds.solution:
Let, the centre of the circle whose locus is to be determined is P(h, k) and it touches y-axis in the first quadrant and we know that if a circle touches the y-axis, then its radius is equal to the absolute value of the x-coordinate of the centre.

Hence, the radius of the circle is =h.

Also, we know that if two circles touches externally, then the distance between their centres is equal to the sum of their radii.

And, the centre and radius of a circle x2+y2=r2 is 0, 0 and 1 respectively.

Now, since the circle with centre h, k and radius h touches x2+y2=1externally, then

⇒h+1=h2+k2

Squaring both the sides, we get

⇒h2+2h+1=h2+k2

⇒k2=2h+1

Now, to get the locus, replace h, k by x, y, to get

y2=2x+1.

Hence, the required locus is y2=2x+1

⇒y=1+2x, x≥0.

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: Tangents drawn from the point $(-8,0)$ to the parabola $y^2=8 x$ touch the parabola at $P$ and $Q$. If $F$ is the focus of the parabola, then the area of the triangle $P F Q$ (in sq. units) is equal to
questionIds.solution:
Equation of the chord of contact $P Q$ is given by:
$$
T=0
$$
or $T \equiv y y_1-4\left(x+x_1\right)$,
where $\left(x_1, y_1\right) \equiv(-8,0)$
$\therefore$ Equation becomes: $x=8$
$\&$ Chord of contact is $x=8$
$\therefore$ Coordinates of point $P$ and $Q$ are $(8,8)$ and $(8,-8)$
and focus of the parabola is $F(2,0)$
$\therefore$ Area of triangle $P Q F=\frac{1}{2} \times(8-2) \times(8+8)$ $=48$ sq. units

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q:
If both the roots of the quadratic equation x2-mx+4=0 are real and distinct and they lie in the interval 1, 5, then m lies in the interval:

Note: In the actual JEE paper interval was 1, 5
questionIds.solution:
Given roots of x2-mx+4=0 are distinct and lies in 1, 5, Following conditions must be true

(i) D>0⇒m2-16>0

⇒m-4m+4>0

⇒m∈-∞, -4∪4, ∞

(ii) f1>0⇒m<5

(iii) f5>0⇒m<295 

(iv) 1<-b2a<5⇒2<m<10

Taking intersection of all conditions, we get m∈4, 5

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: The angle between the straight lines, whose direction cosines <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>l</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>m</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>n</mi></math> are given by the equations <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>l</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>m</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>n</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi><mi>n</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>n</mi><mi>l</mi><mo>+</mo><mo>lm</mo><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>, is:
questionIds.solution:
Given equations of direction cosies

2ℓ+2m-n=0 ...............i
mn+nℓ+ℓm=0..............ii
ℓm+n(ℓ+m)=0

From equation i

n=2ℓ+m
ℓm+2(ℓ+m)2=0
2ℓ2+2m2+5ℓm=0

Dividing by m2 on both sides
2ℓm2+2+5ℓm=0
Let ℓm=t
2t2+5t+2=0

2t2+4t+t+2=0

t+22t+1=0
t=-2,-12

Case 1

ℓm=−12

m=−2ℓ, n=-2ℓ

(ℓ,−2ℓ,−2ℓ)⇒(1,−2,−2)

Case 2

ℓm=-2

ℓ=-2m, n=-2m

-2m,m,-2m⇒-2,1,-2

cosθ=1×-2+-2×1+-2×-212+-22+-22-22+12+-22

cosθ=-2-2+49=0

θ=π2

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: Which of the following points lies on the tangent to the curve <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>4</mn></msup><msup><mi>e</mi><mi>y</mi></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><msqrt><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></msqrt><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn></math> at the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>)</mo></math>?
questionIds.solution:
eyy'x4+4x3ey+2y'12y+1=0

at (1,0)
y'+4+y'=0  ⇒  y'=-2
equation of tangent at (1,0) is 2 x+y-2=0
so option (3) is correct

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: If ${ }^n C_r$ denotes the number of combination of $n$ things taken $r$ at a time, then the expression ${ }^n C_{r+1}+{ }^n C_{r-1}+2 x^n C_r$ equals
questionIds.solution:
After t; velocity $=\mathrm{f} \times \mathrm{t}$
$V_{B A}=\vec{f} t+(-\vec{u})=\sqrt{f^2 t^2+u^2-2 f u t \cos \alpha}$
For max. and min.
$\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dt}}\left(\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{BA}}^2\right)=2 \mathrm{f}^2 \mathrm{t}-2 \mathrm{fu} \cos \alpha=0 \quad$ or $\quad \mathrm{t}=\frac{\mathrm{u} \cos \alpha}{\mathrm{f}}$
Therefore, total no. of values of $\mathrm{r}=33$.

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: The distance of the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>9</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> from the point of intersection of the line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow><mn>1</mn></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow><mn>2</mn></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi>z</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow><mn>2</mn></mfrac></math> and the plane <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>z</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>17</mn></math> is:
questionIds.solution:
Let x-31=y-42=z-52=t

⇒ x=3+t, y=2t+4, z=2t+5

For a point of intersection with x+y+z=17, then it satisfies the equation of a plane. 

⇒3+t+2 t+4+2 t+5=17

⇒ 5t=5⇒t=1

⇒ The point of intersection is 4,6,7.

Then, distance between 1,1,9 and 4,6,7 is 9+25+4=38.

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: Consider <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn></math> observation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>x</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><msub><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><mo>.</mo><mo>..</mo><msub><mi>x</mi><mn>10</mn></msub></math>, such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><munderover><mo>∑</mo><mrow><mi>i</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow><mn>10</mn></munderover><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi>x</mi><mi>i</mi></msub><mo>−</mo><mi>α</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><munderover><mo>∑</mo><mrow><mi>i</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow><mn>10</mn></munderover><msup><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi>x</mi><mi>i</mi></msub><mo>−</mo><mi>β</mi></mrow></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>40</mn></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>β</mi></math> are positive integers. Let the mean and the variance of the observations be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>6</mn><mn>5</mn></mfrac></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>84</mn><mn>25</mn></mfrac></math> respectively. The <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>β</mi><mi>α</mi></mfrac></math> is equal to:
questionIds.solution:
Given observations are x1,x2,...,x10

⇒∑i=110xi−α=2

⇒∑i=110xi−10α=2

Mean, μ=65=∑xi10

⇒∑xi=12

⇒10α+2=12

⇒α=1

Now, ∑i=110xi−β2=40. Let yi=xi−β

⇒σy2=110∑yi2−y¯2

⇒8425=110∑xi−β2−∑i=110xi−β102

⇒8425=4−12−10β102

⇒6−5β52=4−8425

⇒6−5β52=1625

⇒6−5β=±4

⇒β=25 (not possible) or β=2

Hence, βα=2

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q:
Let S be the set of all λ∈R for which the system of linear equations

2x-y+2z=2

x-2y+λz=-4

x+λy+z=4

has no solution. Then the set S
questionIds.solution:
For no solution Δ=0 and at least one of Δ1,Δ2,Δ3 is non-zero.
Δ=2-121-2λ1λ1=-λ-12λ+1
Δ1=2-12-4-2λ4λ1=-2λ2+6λ-4
Δ=0⇒  λ=1,-12

 And for  λ=1,-12,  ∆1≠0
Hence, S=1,-12

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q:
Two lines $\frac{x-3}{1}=\frac{y+1}{3}=\frac{z-6}{-1}$ and $\frac{x+5}{7}=\frac{y-2}{-6}=\frac{z-3}{4}$

intersect at the point $R$. The reflection of $R$ in the $x y$ - plane has coordinates:
questionIds.solution:
Let the coordinate of $P$ with respect to line
$\frac{x-3}{1}=\frac{y+1}{3}=\frac{z-6}{-1}=\lambda$ $\frac{x+5}{7}=\frac{y-2}{-6}=\frac{z-3}{4}=\mu$ $L_{1}=(\lambda+3,3 \lambda-1,-\lambda+6)$ and coordinate of $P$ w.r.t. line $L_{2}=(7 \mu-5,-6 \mu+2,4 \mu+3)$ $\therefore \quad \lambda-7 \mu=-8,3 \lambda+6 \mu=3, \lambda+4 \mu=3$ From above equation : $\lambda=-1, \mu=1$ $\therefore \quad$ Coordinate of point of intersection $R=(2,-4,7)$ Image of $R$ w.r.t. $x y$ plane $=(2,-4,-7)$

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: The maximum value of the term independent of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi></math> in the expansion of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced><mrow><mi>t</mi><msup><mi>x</mi><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>5</mn></mfrac></msup><mo>+</mo><mfrac><msup><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>-</mo><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>10</mn></mfrac></msup><mi>t</mi></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mn>10</mn></msup></math>where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>∈</mo><mfenced><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> is:

Tr+1=Cr10tx1/510-r1-x110tr

=Cr10t10-2rx10-r51-xr10

For the term independent of t, 

⇒10-2r=0

⇒r=5

T6=fx=C510x1-x; for maximum

f'x=0⇒x=23&f''23<0

so fxmax.=C51023·13

=2.10!335!2

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: Marks obtains by all the students of class 12 are presented in a freqency distribution with classes of equal width. Let the median of this grouped data be 14 with median class interval 12-18 and median class frequency 12 . If the number of students whose marks are less than 12 is 18 , then the total number of students is
questionIds.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text { median }=\ell+\left(\frac{\frac{\mathrm{N}}{2}-\mathrm{F}}{\mathrm{f}}\right) \times \mathrm{h} \\ & =12+\left(\frac{\frac{\mathrm{N}}{2}-18}{12}\right) \times 6=14 \\ & \Rightarrow\left(\frac{\frac{\mathrm{~N}}{2}-18}{12}\right) \times 6=2 \\ & \frac{\mathrm{~N}}{2}-18=4 \Rightarrow \mathrm{~N}=44\end{aligned}$

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: A seven digit number is formed using digits <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>5</mn></math>. The probability, that number so formed is divisible by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn></math>, is
questionIds.solution:
Digits =3,3,4,4,4,5,5

Total 7 digit numbers =7!2!2!3!

Number of 7 digit number divisible by 2 

⇒last digit =4

Now 7 digit numbers which are divisible by 2

=6!2!2!2!
Required probability =6!2!2!2!7!3!2!2!=37

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: If $\alpha \neq \beta$ but $\alpha^2=5 \alpha-3$ and $\beta^2=5 \beta-3$ then the equation having $\alpha / \beta$ and $\beta / \alpha$ as its roots is
questionIds.solution:
We have $\alpha^2=5 \alpha-3$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow \alpha^2-5 \alpha+3=0 \Rightarrow \alpha=\frac{5 \pm \sqrt{13}}{2} \text {. Similarly, } \beta^2=5 \beta-3 \Rightarrow \alpha=\frac{5 \pm \sqrt{13}}{2} \\
& \therefore \alpha=\frac{5+\sqrt{13}}{2} \text { and } \beta=\frac{5-\sqrt{13}}{2} \text { or vice - versa } \\
& \alpha^2+\beta^2=\frac{50+26}{4}=19 \& \alpha \beta=\frac{1}{4}(25-13)=3
\end{aligned}
$$
Thus, the equation having $\frac{\alpha}{\beta} \& \frac{\beta}{\alpha}$ as its roots is
$$
x^2-x\left(\frac{\alpha}{\beta}+\frac{\beta}{\alpha}\right)+\frac{\alpha \beta}{\alpha \beta}=0 \Rightarrow x^2-x\left(\frac{\alpha^2+\beta^2}{\alpha \beta}\right)+1=0 \text { or } 3 x^2-19 x+1=0
$$

questionIds.q: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfenced open="{" close=""><mtable columnspacing="1.4ex" columnalign="left"><mtr><mtd><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo></mtd><mtd><mi>x</mi><mo>≤</mo><mn>0</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mfenced open="|" close="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo></mtd><mtd><mi>x</mi><mo>></mo><mn>0</mn></mtd></mtr></mtable></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfenced open="{" close=""><mtable columnspacing="1.4ex" columnalign="left"><mtr><mtd><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo></mtd><mtd><mi>x</mi><mo><</mo><mn>0</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><msup><mfenced><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mi>b</mi><mo>,</mo></mtd><mtd><mi>x</mi><mo>≥</mo><mn>0</mn></mtd></mtr></mtable></mfenced></math> are continuous on <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>g</mi><mi>o</mi><mi>f</mi></mrow></mfenced><mfenced><mn>2</mn></mfenced><mo>+</mo><mfenced><mrow><mi>f</mi><mi>o</mi><mi>g</mi></mrow></mfenced><mfenced><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> is equal to:

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q:
Let $S=\left\{\left(\begin{array}{ll}a_{11} & a_{12} \\ a_{21} & a_{22}\end{array}\right): a_{i j} \in\{0,1,2\}, a_{11}=a_{22}\right\}$
Then the number of non-singular matrices in the set $S$ is :
questionIds.solution:
The matrices in the form
$$
\left[\begin{array}{ll}
a_{11} & a_{12} \\
a_{21} & a_{22}
\end{array}\right], a_{i j} \in\{0,1,2\}, a_{11}=a_{12} \text { are }
$$
$$
\left[\begin{array}{cc}
0 & 0 / 1 / 2 \\
0 / 1 / 2 & 0
\end{array}\right],\left[\begin{array}{cc}
1 & 0 / 1 / 2 \\
0 / 1 / 2 & 1
\end{array}\right],\left[\begin{array}{cc}
2 & 0 / 1 / 2 \\
0 / 1 / 2 & 2
\end{array}\right]
$$
At any place, $0 / 1 / 2$ means 0,1 or 2 will be the element at that place.

Hence there are total $27=3 \times 3+3 \times 3+3 \times 3$ ) matrices of the above form. Out of which the matrices which are singular are
$$
\left[\begin{array}{cc}
0 & 0 / 1 / 2 \\
0 & 0
\end{array}\right],\left[\begin{array}{cc}
0 & 0 \\
1 / 2 & 0
\end{array}\right],\left[\begin{array}{ll}
1 & 1 \\
1 & 1
\end{array}\right],\left[\begin{array}{ll}
2 & 2 \\
2 & 2
\end{array}\right]
$$
Hence there are total $7(=3+2+1+1)$ singular matrices.

Therefore number of all non-singular matrices in the given form $=27-7=20$

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: Let the point A be the foot of perpendicular drawn from the point $P(a, b, 0)$ on the line $\dfrac{x-1}{2} = \dfrac{y-2}{1} = \dfrac{z-\alpha}{3}$. If the midpoint of the line segment PA is $\left(0, \dfrac{3}{4}, \dfrac{-1}{4}\right)$, then the value of $a^2 + b^2 + \alpha^2$ is equal to:
questionIds.solution:
Let $P = (a, b, 0)$ and the midpoint of $PA$ be $M = \left(0, \dfrac{3}{4}, -\dfrac{1}{4}\right)$.

Using the midpoint formula, the coordinates of $A$ are given by $2M - P$:

$A = \left(-a, \dfrac{3}{2} - b, -\dfrac{1}{2}\right)$

Since $A$ lies on the given line $\dfrac{x-1}{2} = \dfrac{y-2}{1} = \dfrac{z-\alpha}{3} = \lambda$, we can write:

$\dfrac{-a-1}{2} = \lambda \Rightarrow a = -2\lambda - 1$

$\dfrac{\dfrac{3}{2}-b-2}{1} = \lambda \Rightarrow b = -\lambda - \dfrac{1}{2}$

$\dfrac{-\dfrac{1}{2}-\alpha}{3} = \lambda \Rightarrow \alpha = -3\lambda - \dfrac{1}{2}$

The direction ratios of the line segment $PA$ are the differences in coordinates of $A$ and $P$:

$\vec{PA} = \left(-2a, \dfrac{3}{2} - 2b, -\dfrac{1}{2}\right)$

Since $PA$ is perpendicular to the given line, the dot product of their direction ratios must be zero. The direction ratios of the line are $(2, 1, 3)$.

$2(-2a) + 1\left(\dfrac{3}{2} - 2b\right) + 3\left(-\dfrac{1}{2}\right) = 0$

$-4a + \dfrac{3}{2} - 2b - \dfrac{3}{2} = 0$

$-4a - 2b = 0 \Rightarrow 2a + b = 0$

Substituting the expressions for $a$ and $b$ in terms of $\lambda$:

$2(-2\lambda - 1) + \left(-\lambda - \dfrac{1}{2}\right) = 0$

$-4\lambda - 2 - \lambda - \dfrac{1}{2} = 0$

$-5\lambda - \dfrac{5}{2} = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda = -\dfrac{1}{2}$

Now, substituting $\lambda = -\dfrac{1}{2}$ back to find $a, b,$ and $\alpha$:

$a = -2\left(-\dfrac{1}{2}\right) - 1 = 0$

$b = -\left(-\dfrac{1}{2}\right) - \dfrac{1}{2} = 0$

$\alpha = -3\left(-\dfrac{1}{2}\right) - \dfrac{1}{2} = \dfrac{3}{2} - \dfrac{1}{2} = 1$

Finally, calculating the required value:

$a^2 + b^2 + \alpha^2 = 0^2 + 0^2 + 1^2 = 1$

Answer: $1$

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q:
Consider the following system of questions

αx+2y+z=1

2αx+3y+z=1

3x+αy+2z=β

For some α,β∈ℝ. Then which of the following is NOT correct.
options: It has no solution for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></math> and for all <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>β</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">ℝ</mi></math>

It has no solution for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn></math> and for all <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>β</mi><mo>≠</mo><mn>2</mn></math>

It has a solution for all <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo>≠</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>β</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn></math>
questionIds.solution:
Given,

System of questions

αx+2y+z=1

2αx+3y+z=1

3x+αy+2z=β

Now finding, D=α212α313α2=0

⇒α(6-α)-2(4α-3)+1(2α2-9)=0

⇒α-3(α+1)=0

⇒α=-1,3

Dx=211311α2β=0⇒β=2

⇒2β-2-1(3β-α)+(6-α)=0

⇒β=2

Dy=α112α1132β=0

⇒α(β-2)-1(2αβ-3)+1(4α-3)=0

⇒α(2-β)=0

⇒α=0,β=2

Dz=α212α313αβ=0

⇒α2-αβ-3=0

Now when β=2,α=-1 the system of equations will have infinite solutions,

When α=3, β=2 the system has infinite solution,

And when α=3, β≠2 the system has no solution as Dx & Dy≠0 for β≠2,

Hence, only option B is not correct

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: A dipole comprises of two charged particles of identical magnitude q and opposite in nature. The mass m of the positive charged particle is half of the mass of the negative charged particle. The two charges are separated by a distance l . If the dipole is placed in a uniform electric field E→; in such a way that dipole axis makes a very small angle with the electric field, E→. The angular frequency of the oscillations of the dipole when released is given by:
questionIds.solution:
The data given is 

l=distance between the charges

q=charge

m=mass of the positive charge

2m=mass of negative charge 

The moment of inertia of the system is 

I=m×2mm+2ml2=2ml23

The angular frequency can be written as 

ω=pEI   ...(i)

Substituting the value of moment of inertia in the equation (i) we get 

ω=pE2ml23=3pE2ml2

The value of the dipole moment is

p=ql

Hence, the angular frequency becomes

ω=3qE2ml

This question was given bonus by NTA as none of the options matched in the original paper.

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: Ratio of radius of gyration of a hollow sphere to that of a solid cylinder of equal mass, for moment of Inertia about their diameter axis $\mathrm{AB}$ as shown in figure is $\sqrt{8 / x}$. The value of $x$ is :
questionIds.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{I}_{\text {sphere }}=\frac{2}{3} \mathrm{MR}^2=\mathrm{Mk}_1^2 \\ & \mathrm{I}_{\text {cylinder }}=\frac{1}{12} \mathrm{M}\left(4 \mathrm{R}^2\right)+\frac{1}{4} \mathrm{MR}^2+\mathrm{M}(2 \mathrm{R})^2 \\ & =\frac{67}{12} \mathrm{MR}^2=\mathrm{Mk}_2^2 \\ & \frac{\mathrm{k}_1}{\mathrm{k}_2}=\sqrt{\frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{12}{67}}=\sqrt{\frac{8}{67}}\end{aligned}$

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q:
A 2 kg steel rod of length 0.6 m is clamped on a table vertically at its lower end and is free to rotate in the vertical plane. The upper end is pushed so that the rod falls under gravity. Ignoring the friction due to clamping at its lower end, the speed of the free end of the rod when it passes through its lowest position is _______m s-1.
(Take g=10 m s-2 )
questionIds.solution:
In the above case the torque due to weight of the rod, here use the concept of translation work done and rotational kinetic energy, 

⇒W=K.E.⇒mgl=12Iω2, now the moment of inertia of the rod when the axis of rotation passes through one end of the rod, I=1×ml23,
⇒mgl=12×ml23ω2
⇒ω=6gl, now use the relation between linear velocity and angular velocity, 
⇒v=lω=3.6g=6 m s-1

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: Two light beams of intensities <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mi>I</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn><mi>I</mi></math> interfere on a screen. The phase difference between these beams on the screen at point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> is zero and at point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>π</mi></math>. The difference of resultant intensities, at the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math>, will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi></math>.
questionIds.solution:
The resultant intensity is given by Inet =I1+I2+2I1I2cosϕ

Now, for Imax, phase angle is ϕ=0 and for Imin, phase angle is ϕ=π.

Thus, Imax=I1+I22=9I+4I2=25I

And Imin=I1-I22=9I-4I2=I

The difference of resultant intensities is Imax-Imin=25I-I=24I

questionIds.q: At time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> a particle starts travelling from a height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>7</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">z</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> in a plane keeping <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>z</mi></math> coordinate constant. At any instant of time, it's position along the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi></math> directions are defined as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mi>t</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><msup><mi>t</mi><mn>3</mn></msup></math> respectively. questionIds.solution:

Now the acceleration of the particle will be,

At t=1 s⇒d2rdt2=30j^

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: The total charge enclosed in an incremental volume of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>9</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mn>3</mn></msup></math> located at the origin is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><menclose notation="bottom"><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo></menclose></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>nC</mi><mo>,</mo></math> if electric flux density of its field is found as<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>D</mi><mo>=</mo><msup><mi>e</mi><mrow><mo mathvariant="italic">-</mo><mi>x</mi></mrow></msup><mi>sin</mi><mi>y</mi><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><msup><mi>e</mi><mrow><mo mathvariant="italic">-</mo><mi>x</mi></mrow></msup><mi>cosy</mi><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">z</mi><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>
questionIds.solution:
Electric flux density,

D→= charge  Area ×r^=Q4πr2r^=ϵ0Q4πϵ0r2r^

⇒E→=D→ϵ0=e-xsinyi^-e-xcos(j^+2zk)^ϵ0

Also, by Gauss's law,

ρϵ0=∂∂xi^+∂∂yj^+∂∂zk^·E→=∂∂xi^+∂∂yj^+∂∂zk^·D→ϵ0

⇒ρ=∂∂xe-xsiny+∂∂y-e-xcosy+∂∂z(2z)

ρ=-e-xsiny+e-xsiny+2
At origin ρ=-e-0sin0+e-0sin0+2
ρ=2 C m-3

Charge =ρ× volume =2×2×10-9=4×10-9=
4 nC

questionIds.q:

40-T=4a     ⋯1

Now, maximum value of friction force will be,

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: Two satellite $A$ and $B$ go round a planet in circular orbits having radii $4 R$ and $R$ respectively. If the speed of $\mathrm{A}$ is $3 v$, the speed of $\mathrm{B}$ will be :
questionIds.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathbf{v}=\sqrt{\frac{G M}{R}} \\ & \frac{v_A}{v_B}=\sqrt{\frac{R_B}{R_A}}=\sqrt{\frac{R}{4 R}}=\frac{1}{2} \\ & v_B=2 v_A=6 v\end{aligned}$

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>γ</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> be the ratio of molar specific heat at constant pressure and molar specific heat at constant volume of a monoatomic gas and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>γ</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> be the similar ratio of diatomic gas. Considering the diatomic gas molecule as a rigid rotator, the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>γ</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><msub><mi>γ</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mfrac></math> is:
questionIds.solution:
As we know, Cv=fR2

and Cp=Cv+R=fR2+R

Therefore, γ=CpCv=1+2f

For monatomic gas, f=3 and hence γ1=53

For diatomic gas, f=5 and hence γ2=75

Required ratio, γ1γ2=2521

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questionIds.q: It is found that if a neutron suffers an elastic collinear collision with a deuterium at rest, the fractional loss of its energy is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>P</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi></msub></math>, while for its similar collision with a carbon nucleus at rest, the fractional loss of energy is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>P</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi></msub></math>. The values of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>P</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>P</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi></msub></math> are respectively
questionIds.solution:
Since, the linear momentum is conserved, u=V1+2V2.

e=1=V2-V1u (where e⇒the coefficient of restitution), u=V2-V1

V2=2u3 ;V1=-u3. Initial energy, =12u2. Final energy, =12×1×u29=u218. Fractional change =u22-u218u22=0.88



u=V1+12V2⇒1=V2-V1u⇒u=V2-V1
⇒V2=2u13⇒V1=-11u13.
Change in energy,=u2-11u132×100=0.294

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: On a linear temperature scale $\mathrm{Y}$, water freezes at $-160^{\circ} \mathrm{Y}$ and boils at $-50^{\circ} \mathrm{Y}$. On this $\mathrm{Y}$ scale, a temperature of $340 \mathrm{~K}$ would be read as : (water freezes at $273 \mathrm{~K}$ and boils at $373 \mathrm{~K}$ )
questionIds.solution:
Reading on any scale - LFP
UFP - LFP
$=$ constant for all scales
$\frac{340-273}{373-273}=\frac{{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{y}-(-160)}{-50-(-160)}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{67}{100}=\frac{\mathrm{y}+160}{110}$
$\therefore \quad y=-86.3^{\circ} \mathrm{y}$

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: For <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>z</mi><mo>=</mo><msup><mi>a</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>3</mn></msup><msup><mi>y</mi><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac></msup></math>, where '<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math>' is a constant. If percentage error in measurement of  '<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>' and '<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi></math>' are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>%</mo></math>and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo>%</mo></math>, respectively, then the percentage error for '<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>z</mi></math>' will be _____<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>%</mo></math>.
questionIds.solution:
Given: z=a2x3y12

Relative error is given by 

∆zz=3×∆xx+12×∆yy

Now, maximum percentage error in z is 

∆zz×100=34+122×100

=18%.

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q:
A particle is subjected two simple harmonic motions as :
$\mathrm{x}_1=\sqrt{7} \sin 5 \mathrm{tcm}$
and $x_2=2 \sqrt{7} \sin \left(5 t+\frac{\pi}{3}\right) \mathrm{cm}$
where x is displacement and $t$ is time in seconds. The maximum acceleration of the particle is $\mathrm{x} \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~ms}^{-2}$. The value of x is :
questionIds.solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{x}_1=\sqrt{7} \sin 5 \mathrm{t} \\ & \mathrm{x}_2=2 \sqrt{7} \sin \left(5 \mathrm{t}+\frac{\pi}{3}\right)\end{aligned}$
From phasor,

$\therefore$ Amplitude of resultant $\mathrm{SHM}=7$
$\begin{aligned} & \phi=\tan ^{-1} \frac{2 \sqrt{7} \times \sqrt{3} / 2}{\sqrt{7}+2 \sqrt{7} \times \frac{1}{2}}=\tan ^{-1} \frac{\sqrt{21}}{2 \sqrt{7}}=\tan ^{-1} \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \\ & \therefore \quad \mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{R}}=7 \sin (5 \mathrm{t}+\phi) \\ & \quad \mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{R}}=-7 \times 25 \sin (5 \mathrm{t}+\phi) \\ & \therefore \mathrm{a}_{\max }=175 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{sec}=175 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{sec}\end{aligned}$

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q:
$\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}$ and C are disc, solid sphere and spherical shell respectively with same radii and masses. These masses are placed as shown in figure.

The moment of inertia of the given system about $P Q$ is $\frac{X}{15} I$, where $I$ is the moment of inertia of the disc about its diameter. The value of x is _______.
questionIds.solution:
All bodies have same mass and same radius.
$\mathrm{A} \rightarrow$ Disc
$\mathrm{B} \rightarrow$ Solid sphere
$\mathrm{C} \rightarrow$ Spherical shell
and, $\mathrm{I}=\frac{\mathrm{MR}^2}{4}$
$\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{PQ}}=\frac{\mathrm{MR}^2}{4}+\left(\frac{2}{5} \mathrm{MR}^2+\mathrm{MR}^2\right)+\left(\frac{2}{3} \mathrm{MR}^2+\mathrm{MR}^2\right)$
$\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{PQ}}=\frac{15 \mathrm{MR}^2+24 \mathrm{MR}^2+60 \mathrm{MR}^2+40 \mathrm{MR}^2+60 \mathrm{MR}^2}{60}$
$\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{PQ}}=\frac{199}{60} \mathrm{MR}^2=\frac{199}{15}\left(\frac{\mathrm{MR}^2}{4}\right)$
$=\frac{199}{15} \mathrm{I}$

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: To find the spring constant $(k)$ of a spring experimentally, a student commits $2 \%$ positive error in the measurement of time and $1 \%$ negative error in measurement of mass. The percentage error in determining value of $k$ is :
questionIds.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{T}=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{k}}} \\ & \mathrm{T}^2 \propto \frac{\mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{k}} \\ & \frac{2 \Delta \mathrm{T}}{\mathrm{T}} \%=\frac{\Delta \mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{m}} \%-\frac{\Delta \mathrm{k}}{\mathrm{k}} \% \\ & \frac{\Delta \mathrm{k}}{\mathrm{k}} \%=\frac{\Delta \mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{m}} \%-\frac{2 \Delta \mathrm{T}}{\mathrm{T}} \% \\ & \frac{\Delta \mathrm{k}}{\mathrm{k}} \%=(-1) \%-2(2) \%=|-5 \%|=5 \%\end{aligned}$

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: For an amplitude modulated wave the minimum amplitude is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>, while the modulation index is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>%</mo></math>. The maximum amplitude of the modulated wave is:
questionIds.solution:
Modulatio index is the ratio of the difference in amplitudes to the sum of the amplitudes.

The formula to calculate the modulation index of a wave is given by

μ=Amax-AminAmax+Amin   ...1

Substitute the values of the known parameters into equation (1) and solve to calculate the required value of maximum amplitude.

0.6=Amax-3Amax+3⇒0.6Amax+1.8=Amax-3⇒0.4Amax=4.8⇒Amax= 4.80.4=12 V

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: Consider a spherical shell of radius R at temperature T. The black body radiation inside it can be considered as an ideal gas of photons with internal energy per unit volume <math><mi>u</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi>U</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>V</mi></mrow></mfrac><mo>∝</mo><msup><mrow><mi>T</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup></math> and pressure <math><mi>p</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfrac><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mfrac><mrow><mi>U</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>V</mi></mrow></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math> . If the shell now undergoes an adiabatic expansion the relation between T and R is:
questionIds.solution:
∵  in an adiabatic process.

Q=0

So by first law of thermodynamics

Q=dU+W

⇒0=dU+W

⇒W=-dU

∴W=PdV

⇒PdV=-dU ....(i)

Given that UV∝T4⇒U=kVT4

⇒dU=kdVT4=KT4dV+4T3V dT

Also, P=13UV=13kVT4V=KT43 

Putting these values in equation (i)

⇒KT43dV=-kT4dV+4T3V dT

⇒TdV3=-TdV-4VdT

⇒4T3dV=-4VdT

⇒13dVV=-dTT

⇒13ln⁡V=-ln⁡T ⇒ln⁡V=ln⁡T-3

⇒VT3=constant

43πR3 T3=constant

RT=constant

⇒T∝1R

questionIds.subject: Chemistry
questionIds.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questionIds.q: . Identify the correct order of standard enthalpy of formation of sodium halides.
questionIds.solution:
The lattice energy of an ionic compound is the energy required to separate one mole of the compound into its constituent ions in the gas phase.

 Lattice energy∝1r++r-

The lattice energy is inversely proportional to the sum of the ionic radii r++r- of the ions involved. When the size of the ions increases, the distance between them also increases, leading to a decrease in the attractive forces between the ions and a decrease in the lattice energy. Therefore, if the size of the ions increases, the lattice energy generally decreases.

So Order : NaF > NaCl > NaBr > NaI

questionIds.subject: Chemistry
questionIds.q:
Consider the following electrochemical cell at 298 K
$\mathrm{Pt}\left|\mathrm{HSnO}_{2}{ }^{-}(\mathrm{aq})\right| \mathrm{Sn}(\mathrm{OH})_{6}{ }^{2-}(\mathrm{aq})\left|\mathrm{OH}^{-}(\mathrm{aq})\right| \mathrm{Bi}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}(\mathrm{~s}) \mid \mathrm{Bi}(\mathrm{s})$.
If the reaction quotient at a given time is $10^{6}$, then the cell EMF ($\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}$) is $\_\_\_\_$ $\times 10^{-1} \mathrm{~V}$ (Nearest integer).
Given the standard half-cell reduction potential as
$\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Bi}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3} / \mathrm{Bi}, \mathrm{OH}^{-}}^{\circ}=-0.44 \mathrm{~V} \text { and } \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Sn}(\mathrm{OH})_{6}^{2-} / \mathrm{HSnO}_{2}^{-}, \mathrm{OH}^{-}}^{\circ}=-0.90 \mathrm{~V}$
questionIds.solution:
The electrochemical cell has cathode:
$Bi_2O_3 + 3H_2O + 6e^- \rightarrow 2Bi + 6OH^-$ with $E° = -0.44$ V
and anode:
$Sn(OH)_6^{2-} - 2e^- \rightarrow HSnO_2^- + H_2O + OH^-$ with $E° = -0.90$ V.
The standard cell EMF is $E°_{cell} = -0.44 - (-0.90) = 0.46$ V.
Using the Nernst equation with $Q = 10^6$ and $n = 6$ electrons transferred:
$E_{cell} = 0.46 - \frac{0.059}{6} \log(10^6) = 0.46 - \frac{0.059 \times 6}{6} = 0.46 - 0.059 = 0.401$ V.
Expressing as $x \times 10^{-1}$ V:
$0.401 = 4.01 \times 10^{-1}$ V, so $x = 4$ (nearest integer).

questionIds.q: A set of solutions is prepared using <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>180</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of water as a solvent and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of different non-volatile solutes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>. The relative lowering of vapour pressure in the presence of these solutes are in the order [Given, molar mass of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mo>;</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mo>;</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>000</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>]
questionIds.solution:
Number of moles = wtgmMW=10MW

Relative lowering in vapour pressure depends on no. of mole of solute greater the no. of moleof solute greater in RLVP and smaller will be vapour pressure.

So order of vapour pressure is A>B>C.

questionIds.subject: Chemistry
questionIds.q: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>KMnO</mi><mn>4</mn></msub></math> oxidises <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi><mo>-</mo></msup></math> in acidic and neutral/faintly alkaline solution, respectively to
questionIds.solution:
In the acidic medium, MnO4- convert to Mn2+ and I- convert to I2.

2MnO4-+10I-+16H+→2Mn2++5I2+8H2O

In neutral/faintly alkaline solution, MnO4- convert to MnO2 and I- convert to IO3-.

2MnO4-+I-+H2O→2MnO2+2OH-+IO3-

questionIds.subject: Chemistry
questionIds.q:
Given below are two statements :

Statement I: S8 solid undergoes disproportionation reaction under alkaline conditions to form S2− and S2O32−

Statement II: ClO4− can undergo disproportionation reaction under acidic condition.

In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
options: Statement I is correct but statement II is incorrect.
Statement I is incorrect but statement II is correct
Both statement I and statement II are incorrect
Both statement I and statement II are correct
questionIds.solution:
A disproportionation reaction is a reaction in which the same element is simultaneously oxidized and reduced. In this type of reaction, a single substance gives two products, one is oxidized while the other is reduced.

S1:S8+12OH⊝→4S2−+2S2O32−+6H2O

S2:ClO4⊝ cannot undergo disproportionation reaction as chlorine is present in its highest oxidation state.

Hence, statement I is correct and II is incorrect.

<math><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math>

questionIds.subject: Chemistry
questionIds.q: The number of paramagnetic species among the following is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><msub><mi>Li</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><msubsup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mn>2</mn><mo>-</mo></msubsup><mo>,</mo><msubsup><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>2</mn><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>-</mo></mrow></msubsup><mo>,</mo><msubsup><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>2</mn><mo>+</mo></msubsup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msubsup><mi>He</mi><mn>2</mn><mo>+</mo></msubsup></math>
questionIds.solution:
The species that contain unpaired electrons are paramagnetic.

Electronic configuration of Li2 molecule is σ1 s2σ*1 s2σ2 s2.

The electronic configuration of B2 molecule is

σ1s2σ⋆1s2σ2s2σ⋆2s2π2px1=π2py1

The presence of unpaired electrons in the two π bonding molecular orbitals explains its paramagnetic nature.

The electronic configuration of C2  KKσ2s2σ*2s2π2px2π2py2

O2: KKσ2 s2σ2 s2σ2pz2π2px2=π2py2π*2px1=π*2py1

Hence, O2 molecule is paramagnetic.

The electronic configuration of C2- is σ1s2σ⋆1s2σ2s2σ⋆2s2π2px2=π2py2σ2pz1

Hence, it is paramagnetic substance.

O22-: KKσ2 s2σ2 s2σ2pz2π2px2=π2py2π*2px2=π*2py2

O2+: KKσ2 s2σ2 s2σ2pz2π2px2=π2py2π*2px1=π*2py

Hence, the above species is paramagnetic.

questionIds.q:
At what pH, given half cell MnO4-(0.1M)∣Mn2+ (0.001 M) will have electrode potential of 1.282 V ? (Nearest Integer)

Given EMnO4-/Mn2+o=1.54 V,2.303RTF=0.059 V
questionIds.solution:
MnO4-+8H++5e-⇌Mn2++4H2O

E=E°-0.0595logMn2+MnO4-H+8

1.282=1.54-0.0595log10-310-1×H+8

0.258×50.059=log10-2H+8

⇒21.86=-2+8pH

pH=2.98

≃3

questionIds.subject: Chemistry
questionIds.q: '$x$' is the product which is obtained by the hydrolysis of prop-1-yne in the presence of mercuric sulphate under dilute acidic medium at $333$ K. '$y$' is the product which is obtained by the reaction of ethane nitrile with methyl magnesium bromide in dry ether followed by hydrolysis. IUPAC name of product obtained from '$x$' and '$y$' in the presence of barium hydroxide followed by heating is :
questionIds.solution:
Hydrolysis of prop-1-yne in the presence of $HgSO_4$ and dilute $H_2SO_4$ yields acetone.

$CH_3-C \equiv CH + H_2O \xrightarrow{Hg^{2+}, H^+} CH_3-CO-CH_3$

Thus, $x$ is acetone.

Reaction of ethane nitrile ($CH_3CN$) with methyl magnesium bromide ($CH_3MgBr$) followed by hydrolysis also yields acetone.

$CH_3-C \equiv N + CH_3MgBr \rightarrow CH_3-C(CH_3)=NMgBr \xrightarrow{H_3O^+} CH_3-CO-CH_3$

Thus, $y$ is acetone.

Reaction of $x$ and $y$ (both acetone) in the presence of $Ba(OH)_2$ followed by heating results in an aldol condensation.

$2 CH_3-CO-CH_3 \xrightarrow{Ba(OH)_2} CH_3-C(OH)(CH_3)-CH_2-CO-CH_3$

Heating causes dehydration to form an unsaturated ketone.

$CH_3-C(OH)(CH_3)-CH_2-CO-CH_3 \xrightarrow{\Delta} CH_3-C(CH_3)=CH-CO-CH_3$

Numbering the principal carbon chain to give the lowest locant to the ketone group, the IUPAC name of the product is 4-methylpent-3-en-2-one.

Answer: 4-Methylpent-3-en-2-one

questionIds.q: In Kjeldahl's method for estimation of nitrogen, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CuSO</mi><mn>4</mn></msub></math> acts as :
questionIds.solution:
Kjeldahl's method is used for the estimation of nitrogen. The organic compound is heated with conc. H2SO4 in presence of K2SO4 and a little CuSO4 to convert all the nitrogen into NH42SO4.K2SO4 raises the boiling point of H2SO4 while CuSO4 catalyses the reaction.

Hence, B is the answer.

questionIds.subject: Chemistry
questionIds.q:
When 1 g each of compounds AB and $\mathrm{AB}_2$ are dissolved in 15 g of water separately, they increased the boiling point of water by 2.7 K and 1.5 K respectively. The atomic mass of A (in amu) is ____ $\times 10^{-1}$ (Nearest integer)
(Given : Molal boiling point elevation constant is $\left.0.5 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right)$
questionIds.solution:
For AB
$\begin{aligned}
& \Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}=2.7 \mathrm{~K} \\
& 2.7=1 \times 0.5 \times \mathrm{m} \\
& \mathrm{~m}=\frac{27}{5}
\end{aligned}$
Let molar mass of $\mathrm{AB}=\mathrm{x}$.
$\begin{aligned}
& \text { So } \frac{1 / x}{15} \times 1000=\frac{27}{5} \\
& x=12.34
\end{aligned}$
For $\mathrm{AB}_2$
$\begin{aligned}
& \Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}=1.5 \mathrm{~K} \\
& 1.5=1 \times 0.5 \times \mathrm{m} \\
& \mathrm{~m}=3
\end{aligned}$
Let molar mass of $\mathrm{AB}_2=\mathrm{y}$
So $\frac{1 / \mathrm{y}}{15} \times 1000=3$
$\begin{aligned}
& y=\frac{1000}{45} \\
& y=22.22
\end{aligned}$
Now let a and b be atomic masses of A and B respectively, then
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{A}+\mathrm{b}=12.34 \quad...(i)\\
& \mathrm{~A}+2 \mathrm{~b}=22.22 \quad...(ii) \\
& \mathrm{~B}=22.22-12.34=9.88 \\
& \text { Now } \mathrm{a}=12.34-9.88=2.46 \\
& =24.6 \times 10^{-1}=25 \times 10^{-1}
\end{aligned}$

0.36=0.0251lnk

In k=14.4      =144×10-1
