⇒x1=8+3048; x2=8-3048

Similarly, y=10-8±3045=2±3045

⇒y1=2-3045;y2=2+3045

Distance =x1-x22+y1-y22

=4×30464+4×30425

=16915

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi>R</mi></math> and let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>β</mi></math> be the roots of the equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mn>60</mn><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>4</mn></mfrac></msup><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>a</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>α</mi><mn>4</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>β</mi><mn>4</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>30</mn></math>, then the product of all possible values of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math> is _____ .
questionIds.solution:
Since, α,β be the roots of the equation x2+6014x+a=0, therefore

α+β=-6014

αβ=a

Given:

α4+β4=-30

⇒α2+β22-2α2β2=-30

⇒α+β2-2αβ2-2a2=-30

⇒6012-2a2-2a2=-30

⇒ 60+4a2-4a×6012-2a2=-30

⇒ 2a2-4·6012a+90=0

⇒a2-2·6012a+45=0

Product of roots=45

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: In a triangle $\mathrm{PQR}, \angle \mathrm{R}=\frac{\pi}{2}$. If $\tan \left(\frac{\mathrm{P}}{2}\right)$ and $\tan \left(\frac{\mathrm{Q}}{2}\right)$ are the roots of $a x^2+b x+c=0, a \neq 0$ then
questionIds.solution:
$\tan \left(\frac{\mathrm{P}}{2}\right), \tan \left(\frac{\mathrm{Q}}{2}\right)$ are the roots of $\mathrm{ax}^2+\mathrm{bx}+\mathrm{c}=0$
$$
\tan \left(\frac{\mathrm{P}}{2}\right)+\tan \left(\frac{\mathrm{Q}}{2}\right)=-\frac{\mathrm{b}}{\mathrm{a}}
$$
$\tan \left(\frac{\mathrm{P}}{2}\right) \tan \left(\frac{\mathrm{Q}}{2}\right)=\frac{\mathrm{c}}{\mathrm{a}}$
$\frac{\tan \left(\frac{\mathrm{P}}{2}\right)+\tan \left(\frac{\mathrm{Q}}{2}\right)}{1-\tan \left(\frac{\mathrm{P}}{2}\right) \tan \left(\frac{\mathrm{Q}}{2}\right)}=\tan \left(\frac{\mathrm{P}}{2}+\frac{\mathrm{Q}}{2}\right)=1$
$\Rightarrow \frac{-\frac{\mathrm{b}}{\mathrm{a}}}{1-\frac{\mathrm{c}}{\mathrm{a}}}=1 \Rightarrow-\frac{\mathrm{b}}{\mathrm{a}}=\frac{\mathrm{a}}{\mathrm{a}}-\frac{\mathrm{c}}{\mathrm{a}} \Rightarrow-\mathrm{b}=\mathrm{a}-\mathrm{c}$
$\mathrm{c}=\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}$.

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q:
Let $[\mathrm{x}]$ denote the greatest integer less than or equal to $\mathrm{X}$. Then :

$\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{\tan \left(\pi \sin ^{2} x\right)+(|\mathrm{x}|-\sin (x[x]))^{2}}{x^{2}}$
questionIds.solution:
RHL is, $\lim _{x \rightarrow 0^{+}} \frac{\tan \left(\pi \sin ^{2} x\right)+(x-0)^{2}}{x^{2}}$

$=\lim _{x \rightarrow 0^{+}}\left(\frac{\tan \left(\pi \sin ^{2} x\right.}{x^{2}}+1\right)=1+\pi$

And LHL is, $\lim _{x \rightarrow 0^{-}} \frac{\tan \left(\pi \sin ^{2} x\right)+(-x+\sin x)^{2}}{x^{2}}$

$=\lim _{x \rightarrow 0^{-}} \frac{\tan \left(\pi \sin ^{2} x\right)+x^{2}+\sin ^{2} x-2 x \sin x}{x^{2}}$

$=\pi+1+1-2=\pi$

Since, LHL $\neq$ RHL Hence, limit does not exist.

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q:
Statement 1: If the system of equations $x+k y+$ $3 z=0,3 x+k y-2 z=0,2 x+3 y-4 z=0$ has a nontrivial solution, then the value of $k$ is $\frac{31}{2}$.
Statement 2: A system of three homogeneous equations in three variables has a non trivial solution if the determinant of the coefficient matrix is zero.
options: Statement 1 is false, Statement 2 is true.
Statement 1 is true, Statement 2 is true, Statement 2 is a correct explanation for Statement 1.
Statement 1 is true, Statement 2 is true,, Statement 2 is not a correct explanation for Statement 1.
Statement 1 is true, Statement 2 is false.
questionIds.solution:
Given system of equations is
$$
\begin{aligned}
& x+k y+3 z=0 \\
& 3 x+k y-2 z=0 \\
& 2 x+3 y-4 z=0
\end{aligned}
$$
Since, system has non-trivial solution
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \therefore\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
1 & k & 3 \\
3 & k & -2 \\
2 & 3 & -4
\end{array}\right|=0 \\
\Rightarrow & 1(-4 k+6)-k(-12+4)+3(9-2 k)=0 \\
\Rightarrow & 4 k+33-6 k=0 \Rightarrow k=\frac{33}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$
Hence, statement - 1 is false.
Statement- 2 is the property.
It is a true statement.

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: The area enclosed between the curves $y^2=x$ and $y=|x|$ is
questionIds.solution: $A=\int_0^1(\sqrt{x}-x) d x$ $=\left[\frac{2}{3} x^{3 / 2}-\frac{x^2}{2}\right]_0^1$ $=\frac{2}{3}-\frac{1}{2}=\frac{1}{6}$.

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: Let the vertex $A$ of a triangle $ABC$ be $(1, 2)$, and the mid-point of the side $AB$ be $(5, -1)$. If the centroid of this triangle is $(3, 4)$ and its circumcenter is $(\alpha, \beta)$, then $21(\alpha + \beta)$ is equal to:
questionIds.solution:
Let the coordinates of vertex $B$ be $(x_1, y_1)$ and vertex $C$ be $(x_2, y_2)$.

Since the mid-point of $AB$ is $(5, -1)$, we have:
$\dfrac{1 + x_1}{2} = 5 \Rightarrow x_1 = 9$
$\dfrac{2 + y_1}{2} = -1 \Rightarrow y_1 = -4$
Thus, $B \equiv (9, -4)$.

Given the centroid of $\triangle ABC$ is $(3, 4)$, we get:
$\dfrac{1 + 9 + x_2}{3} = 3 \Rightarrow x_2 = -1$
$\dfrac{2 - 4 + y_2}{3} = 4 \Rightarrow y_2 = 14$
Thus, $C \equiv (-1, 14)$.

The circumcenter $(\alpha, \beta)$ is the intersection of the perpendicular bisectors of the sides of the triangle.

For side $AB$, the mid-point is $(5, -1)$ and the slope is $\dfrac{-4 - 2}{9 - 1} = -\dfrac{3}{4}$.
The slope of its perpendicular bisector is $\dfrac{4}{3}$.
Equation of the perpendicular bisector of $AB$ is:
$y - (-1) = \dfrac{4}{3}(x - 5) \Rightarrow 4x - 3y = 23$

For side $AC$, the mid-point is $\left(\dfrac{1 - 1}{2}, \dfrac{2 + 14}{2}\right) = (0, 8)$ and the slope is $\dfrac{14 - 2}{-1 - 1} = -6$.
The slope of its perpendicular bisector is $\dfrac{1}{6}$.
Equation of the perpendicular bisector of $AC$ is:
$y - 8 = \dfrac{1}{6}(x - 0) \Rightarrow x - 6y = -48$

Solving the equations $4x - 3y = 23$ and $x - 6y = -48$:
Multiplying the first equation by $2$ gives $8x - 6y = 46$.
Subtracting the second equation from this gives:
$7x = 94 \Rightarrow x = \dfrac{94}{7}$
Substituting $x$ into the second equation:
$\dfrac{94}{7} - 6y = -48 \Rightarrow 6y = \dfrac{430}{7} \Rightarrow y = \dfrac{215}{21}$

Therefore, the circumcenter is $(\alpha, \beta) = \left(\dfrac{94}{7}, \dfrac{215}{21}\right)$.

Finally, calculating $21(\alpha + \beta)$:
$21(\alpha + \beta) = 21\left(\dfrac{94}{7} + \dfrac{215}{21}\right) = 3(94) + 215 = 282 + 215 = 497$.

Answer: $497$

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: Let the set of all values of $p$, for which $f(x)=\left(p^2-6 p+8\right)\left(\sin ^2 2 x-\cos ^2 2 x\right)+2(2-p) x+7$ does not have any critical point, be the interval $(a, b)$. Then $16 a b$ is equal to _______
questionIds.solution:
$\begin{aligned} & f(x)=-\left(p^2-6 p+8\right) \cos 4 n+2(2-p) n+7 \\ & f^1(x)=+4\left(p^2-6 p+8\right) \sin 4 x+(4-2 p) \neq 0 \\ & \sin 4 x \neq \frac{2 p-4}{4(p-4)(p-2)}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned}
& \sin 4 x \neq \frac{2(p-2)}{4(p-4)(p-2)} \\
& p \neq 2 \\
& \quad \sin 4 x \neq \frac{1}{2(p-4)} \\
& \Rightarrow\left|\frac{1}{2(p-4)}\right|>1
\end{aligned}$
on solving we get
$\therefore \mathrm{p} \in\left(\frac{7}{2}, \frac{9}{2}\right)$
Hence $\mathrm{a}=\frac{7}{2}, \mathrm{~b}=\frac{9}{2}$
$\therefore 16 \mathrm{ab}=252$

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q:
The area (in sq. units) of the region
$\left\{(x, y): 0 \leq \mathrm{y} \leq 2|x|+1,0 \leq \mathrm{y} \leq x^2+1,|x| \leq 3\right\}$ is
questionIds.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text { Area }=2\left[\int_0^2\left(x^2+1\right) d x+\frac{1}{2}[5+7] \times 1\right] \\ & =\frac{64}{3}\end{aligned}$

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: The distance of the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>9</mn><mo>,</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>16</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> from the plane <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>z</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn></math> measure parallel to the line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow><mn>3</mn></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mstyle displaystyle="true"><mn>2</mn><mo>-</mo><mi>y</mi></mstyle><mn>4</mn></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi>z</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow><mn>12</mn></mfrac></math> is
questionIds.solution:
Equation of line passing through -1,9,-16 and parallel to x+43=y-2-4=z-312 is

x+13=y-9-4=z+1612=λ

Any point on this line is P3λ-1, -4λ+9, 12λ-16 

For finding point of intersection of line and a plane, we have

6λ-2-12λ+27-12λ+16=5

⇒λ=2

So, P5,1,8

Required distance is 

=5+12+1-92+8+162

= 36+64+576=26 units

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: The number of integral terms in the expansion of $(\sqrt{3}+8 \sqrt{5})^{256}$ is
questionIds.solution:
$\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{r}+1}={ }^{256} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{r}}(\sqrt{3})^{256-\mathrm{r}}(\sqrt[8]{5})^{\mathrm{r}}={ }^{256} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{r}}(3)^{\frac{256-\mathrm{r}}{2}}(5)^{\mathrm{r} / 8}$
Terms will be integral if $\frac{256-\mathrm{r}}{2} \& \frac{\mathrm{r}}{8}$ both are +ve integer. As $0 \leq \mathrm{r} \leq 256 \therefore \mathrm{r}=0,8,16,24, \ldots \ldots \ldots \ldots 256$
For above values of $\mathrm{r},\left(\frac{256-\mathrm{r}}{2}\right)$ is also an integer.

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q:
Let $f(x)=\frac{x}{\sqrt{a^{2}+x^{2}}}-\frac{d-x}{\sqrt{b^{2}+(d-x)^{2}}}, x \in \mathbb{R}$ wherea, b

and d are non-zero real constants. Then :
options: $f^{\prime}$ is not a continuous function of $x$
$f$ is neither increasing nor decreasing function of $x$
questionIds.solution:
$f(x)=\frac{x}{\sqrt{a^{2}+x^{2}}}-\frac{(d-x)}{\sqrt{b^{2}+(d-x)^{2}}}$

$=\frac{x}{\sqrt{a^{2}+x^{2}}}+\frac{(x-d)}{\sqrt{b^{2}+(x-d)^{2}}}$

$\begin{array}{l}

f^{\prime}(x)=\frac{\sqrt{a^{2}+x^{2}}-\frac{x(2 x)}{2 \sqrt{a^{2}+x^{2}}}}{\left(a^{2}+x^{2}\right)} \\

=\frac{a^{2}+x^{2}-x^{2}}{\left(a^{2}+x^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}+\frac{b^{2}+(x-d)^{2}-(x-d)^{2}}{\left(b^{2}+(x-d)^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}} \\

+\frac{\sqrt{b^{2}+(x-d)^{2}}-\frac{(x-d) 2(x-d)}{2 \sqrt{b^{2}+(x-d)^{2}}}}{\left(a^{2}+(x-d)^{2}\right)} \\

\Rightarrow f^{\prime}(x)>0, \square x \in R

\end{array}$

$\Rightarrow f(x)$ is increasing function.

Hence, $f(x)$ is increasing function

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>  </mo><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfenced close="|" open="|"><mtable><mtr><mtd><mn>2</mn><msup><mi>cos</mi><mn>4</mn></msup><mi>x</mi></mtd><mtd><mn>2</mn><msup><mi>sin</mi><mn>4</mn></msup><mi>x</mi></mtd><mtd><mn>3</mn><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>sin</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mn>3</mn><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><msup><mi>cos</mi><mn>4</mn></msup><mi>x</mi></mtd><mtd><mn>2</mn><msup><mi>sin</mi><mn>4</mn></msup><mi>x</mi></mtd><mtd><msup><mi>sin</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mn>2</mn><msup><mi>cos</mi><mn>4</mn></msup><mi>x</mi></mtd><mtd><mn>3</mn><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><msup><mi>sin</mi><mn>4</mn></msup><mi>x</mi></mtd><mtd><msup><mi>sin</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi></mtd></mtr></mtable></mfenced></math> then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>5</mn></mfrac><msup><mi>f</mi><mo>'</mo></msup><mo>(</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>)</mo></math> is equal to ________.
questionIds.solution:
Given: fx=2cos4x2sin4x3+sin22x3+2cos4x2sin4xsin22x2cos4x3+2sin4xsin22x

Applying R2→R2-R1, R3→R3-R1

⇒fx=2cos4x2sin4x3+sin22x30-303-3

⇒fx=92cos4x2sin4x3+sin22x10-101-1

⇒fx=92cos4x+2sin4x+3+sin22x

Now, differentiating above function we get,

⇒f'x=98cos3x-sinx+8sin3xcosx+2sin2xcos2x2

⇒f'0=90+0+0

⇒f'0=0

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: If $\lim _{\mathrm{t} \rightarrow 0}\left(\int_0^1(3 x+5)^{\mathrm{t}} \mathrm{d} x\right)^{\frac{1}{t}}=\frac{\alpha}{5 \mathrm{e}}\left(\frac{8}{5}\right)^{\frac{2}{3}}$, then $\alpha$ is equal to ________
questionIds.solution:
$\begin{aligned} \frac{\alpha}{5 e} & =\exp \left(\lim _{t \rightarrow 0} \frac{1}{t}\left(\int_0^1(3 x+5)^t d x-1\right)\right) \\ & =\exp \left(\lim _{t \rightarrow 0} \frac{1}{t}\left(\frac{(3 x+5)^{t+1}}{3(t+1)}\right)_0^1-1\right) \\ & =\exp \left(\lim _{t \rightarrow 0} \frac{1}{t}\left(\frac{8^{t+1}-5^{t+1}}{3(t+1)}-1\right)\right) \\ & =\exp \left(\lim _{t \rightarrow 0} \frac{1}{t}\left(\frac{8^{t+1}-5^{t+1}-3 t-3}{3(t+1)}\right)\right) \\ & =\exp \left(\lim _{t \rightarrow 0}\left(\frac{8^{t+1} \cdot \ln 8-5^{t+1} \ln 5-3}{3(t+1)}\right)\right) \\ & =\exp \left(\frac{\ln 8^8-\ln 5^5-3}{5}\right)\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & =\left(\frac{8}{5}\right)^{2 / 3} \frac{\alpha}{5 e}=\exp \left(\frac{\ln \left(\frac{8^8}{5^5}\right)}{5}-1\right) \\ & \Rightarrow\left(\frac{8}{5}\right)^{2 / 3} \frac{\alpha}{5}=\left(\frac{8^8}{5^5}\right)^{1 / 3}=\left(\frac{8^6 \cdot 8^2}{5^3 \cdot 5^2}\right)^{1 / 3}=\frac{64}{5}\left(\frac{8}{5}\right)^{2 / 3} \\ & \Rightarrow \alpha=64\end{aligned}$

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: The number of real solutions of the equation \(x\left(x^2+3|x|+5|x-1|+6|x-2|\right)=0\) is ______.
questionIds.solution:
Given,

xx2+3|x|+5|x-1|+6|x-2|=0

⇒x=0 or x2+3|x|+5|x-1|+6|x-2|=0

We know that sum of all positive terms can never be zero,

So, x2+3|x|+5|x-1|+6|x-2| can never be zero for any value of x,

So there is no value of x which satisfies the above equation
Hence, only solution is x=0, i.e. number of solution is 1 .

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: For <math><mi>x</mi><mo>></mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo></math> let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><msubsup><mo>∫</mo><mn>1</mn><mi>x</mi></msubsup><mfrac><mrow><mi>log</mi><mo>⁡</mo><mi>t</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>+</mo><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfrac><mi> </mi><mi>d</mi><mi>t</mi><mo>.</mo><mi> </mi></math> Then <math><mi>f</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>+</mo><mi>f</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mfrac><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math> is equal to
questionIds.solution:

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q:
Let y=yx be the solution of the differential equation ex1-y2 dx+yxdy=0, y1=-1
Then the value of y32 is equal to:
questionIds.solution:
We have,

ex1-y2 dx+yxdy=0, y1=-1

⇒  ex1-y2 dx=-yxdy

⇒   ∫-y1-y2 dy=∫xexdx

⇒   12∫d1-y21-y2 dy=∫xexdx

⇒ 1-y2=exx-1+c

Now, at x=1,y=-1, then

⇒  0=0+c⇒c=0

∴  1-y2=exx-1

At x=3, then

1-y32=2e3

⇒  1-y32=4e6

⇒  y32=1-4e6

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: Domain of definition of the function $f(x)=\frac{3}{4-x^2}+\log _{10}\left(x^3-x\right)$, is
questionIds.solution: $f(x)=\frac{3}{4-x^2}+\log _{10}\left(x^3-x\right)$ $4-x^2 \neq 0 ; x^3-x>0 ; x \neq \pm \sqrt{4}$ $\therefore \mathrm{D}=(-1,0) \bigcup(1, \infty)-\{\sqrt{4}\}$ $\mathrm{D}=(-1,0) \bigcup(1,2) \bigcup(2, \infty)$

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: Let $y=y(x)$ be the solution of the differential equation $(x+y+2)^2 d x=d y, y(0)=-2$. Let the maximum and minimum values of the function $y=y(x)$ in $\left[0, \frac{\pi}{3}\right]$ be $\alpha$ and $\beta$, respectively. If $(3 \alpha+\pi)^2+\beta^2=\gamma+\delta \sqrt{3}, \gamma, \delta \in \mathbb{Z}$, then $\gamma+\delta$ equals ______
questionIds.solution:
$\frac{d y}{d x}=(x+y+2)^2$ ...(1) $y(0)=-2$
Let $\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y}+2=\mathrm{v}$ $1+\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}$ from (1) $\frac{d v}{d x}=1+v^2$ $\begin{aligned} & \int \frac{d v}{1+v^2}=\int d x \\ & \tan ^{-1}(v)=x+C \\ & \tan ^{-1}(x+y+2)=x+C \\ & \text { at } x=0 y=-2 \Rightarrow C=0 \end{aligned}$ $\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \tan ^{-1}(\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y}+2)=\mathrm{x} \\ & \mathrm{y}=\tan \mathrm{x}-\mathrm{x}-2 \\ & \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=\tan \mathrm{x}-\mathrm{x}-2, \mathrm{x} \in\left[0, \frac{\pi}{3}\right] \end{aligned}$ $\begin{aligned} & f^{\prime}(x)=\sec ^2 x-1>0 \Rightarrow f(x) \uparrow \\ & f_{\min }=f(0)=-2=\beta \\ & f_{\max }=f\left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right)=\sqrt{3}-\frac{\pi}{3}-2=\alpha \end{aligned}$ now $(3 \alpha+\pi)^2+\beta^2=\gamma+\delta \sqrt{3}$ $\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow(3 \alpha+\pi)^2+\beta^2=(3 \sqrt{3}-6)^2+4 \\ & \gamma+\delta \sqrt{3}=67-36 \sqrt{3} \\ & \Rightarrow \gamma=67 \text { and } \delta=-36 \Rightarrow \gamma+\delta=31 \end{aligned}$

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: The number of square matrices of order <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math> with entries from the set <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>{</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>}</mo></math>, such that the sum of all the elements in each row is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn></math> and the sum of all the elements in each column is also <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn></math>, is
questionIds.solution:
Given,

A 5×5 matrix whose each entry is either 0 or 1, is such that sum of entries of each column as well as each row is 1,

Taking a possible case we get,

1000001000001000001000001

Now arranging the above case we get,

1000001000001000001000001→5 ways→4 ways→3 ways→2 ways→1 way because if we fix 1 at first row and first column then in second row first column 1 cannot come and vice-versa.

So total number of such matrices will be 5×4×3×2×1=120

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q:
Let A, B, C be three points whose position vectors respectively are:

a→=i^+4j^+3k^

b→=2i^+αj^+4k^, α∈R

c→=3i^-2j^+5k^

If α is the smallest positive integer for which a→, b→, c→ are non-collinear, then the length of the median, △ABC, through A is:
questionIds.solution:
Let us assume that vectors are collinear so, AB→∥AC→ if 12=α-4-6=12⇒α=1 

Now, for a→, b→, c→ to be non-collinear smallest positive integer will be α=2

So, mid-point of BC=M52, 0, 92

Now, AM=94+16+94=822

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: A homogeneous solid cylindrical roller of radius <math><mi>R</mi></math> and mass <math><mi>M</mi></math> is pulled on a cricket pitch by a horizontal force. Assuming rolling without slipping, angular acceleration of the cylinder is:
questionIds.solution:
F-fr=ma …i

frR=Iα=mR22α …(ii)

For pure rolling

a=αR …(iii)

From i, (ii) and (iii)

F-mR α2=mαR

F=32mR  α

α=2F3mR

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: Two stream of photons, possessing energies equal to twice and ten times the work function of metal are incident on the metal surface successively. The value of ratio of maximum velocities of the photoelectrons emitted in the two respective cases is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>:</mo><mn>3</mn></math> The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is
questionIds.solution:
KEmax=hν-ϕ

12mv2=hν-ϕ

v=2hν-ϕm

Given hν1=2ϕ

hν2=10ϕ

∴ v1v2=hν1-ϕhν2-ϕ

v1v2=2ϕ-ϕ10ϕ-ϕ=13

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: The temperature-entropy diagram of a reversible engine cycle is given in the figure. Its efficiency is
questionIds.solution: $\eta=\frac{\Delta \mathrm{W}}{\mathrm{Q}_{\mathrm{BC}}}=\frac{\frac{\mathrm{S}_0 \mathrm{~T}_0}{2}}{\frac{3 \mathrm{~S}_0 \mathrm{~T}_0}{2}}=1 / 3$

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: Two satellites revolve around a planet in coplanar circular orbits in anticlockwise direction. Their period of revolutions are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn></math> hour and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn></math> hours respectively. The radius of the orbit of nearer satellite is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>3</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The angular speed of the farther satellite as observed from the nearer satellite at the instant when both the satellites are closest is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mi>x</mi></mfrac><mo>rad</mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
questionIds.solution:
T1=1 hour

⇒ω1==2π rad/hour

T2=8 hours

⇒ω2=π4 rad/hour

R1=2×103 km

As T2∝R3

⇒R2R13=T2T12⇒R2R1=182/3=4⇒R=8×103 km

V1=ω1R1=4π×103 km/h

V1=ω2R2=2π×103 km/h

Relative ω=V1-V2R2-R1=2π×1036×103=π3 rad/hour

x=3

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: When a $U^{238}$ nucleus originally at rest, decays by emitting an alpha particle having a speed ' $u$ ', the recoil speed of the residual nucleus is
questionIds.solution:
Applying the principle of conservation of linear momentum
(4) $(\mathrm{u})=(\mathrm{v})(238) \Rightarrow \mathrm{v}=\frac{4 \mathrm{u}}{238}$

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: A particle of mass $\mathrm{m}$ is at rest at the origin at time $\mathrm{t}=0$. It is subjected to a force $\mathrm{F}(\mathrm{t})=\mathrm{F}_0 \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{bt}}$ in the $x$ direction. Its speed $v(t)$ is depicted by which of the following curves?
questionIds.solution:
$\mathrm{F}=\mathrm{F}_0 \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{bt}}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{a}=\frac{\mathrm{F}}{\mathrm{m}}=\frac{\mathrm{F}_0}{\mathrm{~m}} \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{bt}}$ 
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dt}}=\frac{\mathrm{F}_0}{\mathrm{~m}} \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{bt}}$ 
$\quad\int \mathrm{dv}=\int_0^{\mathrm{t}} \frac{\mathrm{F}}{\mathrm{m}} \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{bt}} \mathrm{dt}$
$\Rightarrow v=\frac{F}{m}\left[\frac{-1}{b}\right]\left[e^{-b t}\right]_0^t$
$\Rightarrow \quad v=\frac{F}{m b}\left[e^{-b t}\right]$
$v=0 \text { at } t=0$
and $\quad v \rightarrow \frac{F}{m b}$ as $t \rightarrow \infty$
So, velocity increases continuously and attains a maximum value of $v=\frac{F}{m b}$ as $t \rightarrow \infty$.

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: A wire of resistance $R$ is bent into a triangular pyramid as shown in figure with each segment having same length. The resistance between points $A$ and $B$ is $R / n$. The value of $n$ is :
questionIds.solution:
As $r=\frac{R}{6}$

(As balanced wheat stone bridge is formed) Now, Equivalent resistance between A and B can be written as
$\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{AB}}}=\frac{1}{2 \mathrm{r}}+\frac{1}{2 \mathrm{r}}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{r}}=\frac{2}{\mathrm{r}}$
$\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{AB}}=\frac{\mathrm{R}}{12}$

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: A body is dropped on ground from a height h1 and after hitting the ground, it rebounds to a height h2. If the ratio of velocities of the body just before and after hitting ground is 4, then percentage loss in kinetic energy of the body is x4. The value of x is _____.
questionIds.solution:
The given ratio of the velocities is 

v1v2=4

The percentage loss in kinetic energy is 

mv212-mv222mv212×100=x4⇒v21-v22v21=x400⇒1-v22v21=x400⇒1-116=x400⇒x=375

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q:
Considering Bohr's atomic model for hydrogen atom :
(A) the energy of H atom in ground state is same as energy of $\mathrm{He}^{+}$ion in its first excited state.
(B) the energy of H atom in ground state is same as that for $\mathrm{Li}^{++}$ion in its second excited state.
(C) the energy of H atom in its ground state is same as that of $\mathrm{He}^{+}$ion for its ground state.
(D) the energy of $\mathrm{He}^{+}$ion in its first excited state is same as that for $\mathrm{Li}^{++}$ion in its ground state
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
questionIds.solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{E} \propto \frac{\mathrm{Z}}{\mathrm{n}^2} \\ & \mathrm{Z}_{\mathrm{H}}=1 \quad \mathrm{Z}_{\mathrm{He}^{+}}=2 \quad \mathrm{Z}_{\mathrm{Li}^{+2}}=3\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & 1^{\text {st }} \text { excited state } \Rightarrow \mathrm{n}=2 \\ & 2^{\text {nd }} \text { excited state } \Rightarrow \mathrm{n}=3\end{aligned}$
From the given statements only A \& B are correct.

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: For a particle in uniform circular motion, the acceleration <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover accent="true"><mi>a</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math> at any point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>R</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>θ</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> on the circular path of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> is (when <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>θ</mi></math> is measured from the positive <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-axis and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>v</mi></math> is uniform speed):
questionIds.solution:
Acceleration of a particle in uniform circular motion is directed towards the centre of the circle and its magnitude is v2R. The radius vector isr→=Rcosθi^+Rsinθj^

Therefore, a→=-v2Rr^=v2R−cosθi^−sinθj^

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: A convex lens made of glass (refractive index $=1.5$ ) has focal length 24 cm in air. When it is totally immersed in water (refractive index $=1.33$ ), its focal length changes to
questionIds.solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \frac{1}{8}=\left(\frac{\mu_{\ell}}{\mu_{\mathrm{s}}}-1\right)\left[\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}_1}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}_2}\right] \\
& \frac{1}{24}=(1.5-1)\left[\frac{2}{\mathrm{R}}\right] ...(i)\\
& \frac{1}{\mathrm{f}^{\prime}}=\left(\frac{1.5}{1.33}-1\right)\left(\frac{2}{\mathrm{R}}\right) \\
& \frac{1}{\mathrm{f}^{\prime}}=\left(\frac{1.5 \times 3}{4}-1\right) \frac{2}{\mathrm{R}}...(ii)
\end{aligned}$
(i) divided by (ii)
$\begin{aligned}
& \frac{\mathrm{f}^{\prime}}{24}=4 \\
& \mathrm{f}^{\prime}=96 \mathrm{~cm}
\end{aligned}$

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q:
Consider a cylindrical tank of radius 1 m is filled with water. The top surface of water is at 15 m from the bottom of the cylinder. There is a hole on the wall of cylinder at a height of 5 m from the bottom. A force of 5×105 N is applied an the top surface of water using a piston. The speed of efflux from the hole will be :
(given atmospheric pressure PA=1.01×105 Pa, density of water ρw=1000 kg m-3 and gravitational acceleration g=10 m s-2)
questionIds.solution:
Assuming there is no atmospheric pressure on piston, P1=FπR2 and P2=P0.

Applying Bernoulli's equation at point 1 and 2,

p1+ρgh1+12ρv12=p2+ρgh2+12ρv22

⇒FπR2+ρgh1-h2=P0+12ρv22

⇒v2=2FπR2ρ-2P0ρ+2gh1-h2

v2=2×5×1053.14×12×1000-2×1.01×1051000+2×10×10

≈17.8 m s-1

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q:
A block of mass m slides on the wooden wedge, which in turn slides backward on the horizontal surface. The acceleration of the block with respect to the wedge is:
Given m=8 kg, M=16 kg
Assume all the surfaces shown in the figure to be frictionless.
questionIds.solution:
Ncos60°=Ma1=16a1
⇒N=32a1
⊥ to incline

N=8gcos30°-8a1sin30°⇒32a1=443g-4a1

⇒  a2=39g

Along incline

8gsin30°+8a1cos30°=ma2=8a2

a2=g×12+39g·32=2g3

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: The decay constants of a radioactive substance for $\alpha$ and $\beta$ emission are $\lambda_\alpha$ and $\lambda_\beta$ respectively. If the substance emits $\alpha$ and $\beta$ simultaneously, then the average half life of the material will be
questionIds.solution:
$T_{a v}=\frac{T_\alpha T_\beta}{T_\alpha+T_\beta}$
If $\alpha$ and $B$ are emitted simultaneously.

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: In an experiment of measuring the refractive index of a glass slab using travelling microscope in physics lab, a student measures real thickness of the glass slab as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>25</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> and apparent thickness of the glass slab at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>00</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math>. Travelling microscope has <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn></math> divisions in one <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cm</mi></math> on main scale and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn></math> divisions on Vernier scale is equal to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>49</mn></math> divisions on main scale. The estimated uncertainty in the measurement of refractive index of the slab is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mn>10</mn></mfrac><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is ______
questionIds.solution:
Given: 

50 VSD=49MSD⇒1VSD=4950MSD

So least count will be

=1MSD-1VSD=120 cm-4950·120 cm

=150×120 cm=0.01 mm

As we know, μ=hh'=5.255.00

Applying log on both sides, we get

lnμ=lnh–lnh’

Differentiating both sides, we get

dμμ=dhh-dh'h'

For calculation, all errors will be added, therefore

dμ=0.015.25+0.015.00μ

=4110×10-3

Hence, x=41

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: The energy required to remove the electron from a singly ionized Helium atom is $2.2$ times the energy required to remove an electron from Helium atom. The total energy required to ionize the Helium atom completely is:
questionIds.solution:
Energy required to remove $\mathrm{e}^{-}$from singly ionized helium atom $=\frac{(13.6) Z^2}{1^2}=54.4 \mathrm{eV}$ $(\because \mathrm{Z}=2)$ Energy required to remove $\mathrm{e}^{-}$from helium atom $=x \mathrm{eV}$
According to question, $54.4 \mathrm{eV}=2.2 \mathrm{x} \Rightarrow \mathrm{x}$ $=24.73 \mathrm{eV}$
Therefore, energy required to ionize helium atom $=(54.4+24.73) \mathrm{eV}=79.12 \mathrm{eV}$

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q:
A stone tied to 180 cm long string at its end is making 28 revolutions in horizontal circle in every minute. The magnitude of acceleration of stone is 1936x m s-2. The value of x ______.

[Take π=227]
questionIds.solution:
Angular velocity, ω=28×2π60 rad s-1.

Required centripetal acceleration is given by,

a=ω2R

=5660×2272×1.8

=(44)2225×1.8=1936125

Hence, required x=125.

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: The time period of a satellite in a circular orbit of the radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The period of another satellite in a circular orbit of the radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn><mi>R</mi></math> is:
questionIds.solution:
T2∝R3.
T'T2=9RR3
 T2=T2×93
 T'=T×33
 T'=27 T

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: Taking the wavelength of first Balmer line in hydrogen spectrum <math><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi></math> to <math><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>)</mo></math> as <math><mn>660</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> , the wavelength of the <math><msup><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi></mrow></msup></math> Balmer line <math><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mo>=</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mn>4</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi></math> to <math><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mo>=</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mn>2</mn><mo>)</mo></math> will be :
questionIds.solution:
From Rydberg’s equation,
1λ=R1n2-1n22
1660×10-9=R122-132
1λ=R122-142
λ600×10-9=59×4×16×412
λ=488.9 nm

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: In a screw gauge, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math> complete rotations of the screw cause it to move a linear distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>25</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. There are<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn></math> circular scale divisions. The thickness of a wire measured by this screw gauge gives a reading of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn></math> main scale divisions and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn></math> circular scale divisions. Assuming negligible error, the thickness of the wire is
questionIds.solution:
The Least count=0.255×100 cm=5×10-4 cm.

Reading =4×0.05 cm+30×5×10-4 cm
              =0.2+0.0150 cm=0.2150 cm

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: <math><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math> grams of steam at <math><msup><mrow><mn>100</mn></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> is mixed with <math><mn>200</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of ice at its melting point in a thermally insulated container. If it produces liquid water at <math><msup><mrow><mn>40</mn></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> [heat of vaporization of water is <math><mn>540</mn><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></mrow><mo>/</mo><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mrow></mrow></math> and heat of fusion of ice is <math><mn>80</mn><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></mrow><mo>/</mo><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mrow></mrow></math> ], the value of <math><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math> is ________
questionIds.solution:
MiceLf+Mice40-0Cw=MsteamLv+Msteam100-40Cw
⇒20080+401=M540+601
⇒200120=M600
M=40 gm

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: A cylindrical wire of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> and conductivity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>7</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> is subjected to an electric field of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mV</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> The expected value of current in the wire will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>3</mn></msup><mi>π</mi><mo> </mo><mi>mA</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _________.
questionIds.solution:
Conductivity σ=5×107 S m-1

Radius r=0.5 mm=5×10-4 m

E=10×10-3 V m-1

J=σE=10×10-3×5×107

⇒J=5×105

⇒iA=5×105

i=5×105×πr2

=5×105×π×5×10-42

=125π×10-3 A

Therefore, i=125 π mA

Hence, x=5

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: A body starts moving from rest with constant acceleration covers displacement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>S</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> in first <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>p</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>)</mo></math> seconds and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>S</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> in first <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi></math> seconds. The displacement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>S</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>+</mo><msub><mi>S</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> will be made in time :
questionIds.solution:
For the displacement S1 of the body, it can be written that

S1=12a(p-1)2   ...1

And, for the displacement S2 of the body, it follows that

S2=12a(p)2   ...2

If t be the required time, it follows that

S1+S2=12at2   ...3

Equations (1), (2) and (3) imply that

(p-1)2+p2=t2⇒t=2p2-2p+1 s

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: The displacement of a particle varies according to the relation $x=4(\cos \pi t+\sin \pi t)$. The amplitude of the particle is
questionIds.solution:
$\left.x=4(\cos \pi t+\sin \pi t)=4\left[\sin \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\pi t\right)\right]+\sin \pi t\right]=4\left[2 \times \sin \left(\frac{\pi t-\frac{\pi}{2}-\pi t}{2}\right) \cos \left(\frac{\pi \mathrm{t}-\frac{\pi}{2}+\pi \mathrm{t}}{2}\right)\right]$
$=8\left[\sin \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot \cos \left(-\frac{\pi}{4}+\pi t\right)\right]$
$=\frac{8}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot \cos \left[\pi t-\frac{\pi}{4}\right]=4 \sqrt{2} \cos \left[\pi t-\frac{\pi}{4}\right]$
Comparing it with standard equation
$X=A \cos (w t-K x) \quad \Rightarrow A=4 \sqrt{2}$

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: An atom absorbs a photon of wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>500</mn><mo> </mo><mi>nm</mi></math> and emits another photon of wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>600</mn><mo> </mo><mi>nm</mi></math>. The net energy absorbed by the atom in this process is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup><mi>eV</mi></math>. The value of<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo> </mo><mi>n</mi><mo> </mo></math>is [Assume the atom to be stationary during the absorption and emission process] (Take <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>34</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"/> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>c</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>8</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> ).
questionIds.solution:
The energy of a photon is given by E=hcλ.

It is given that λ1=500 nm, λ2=600 nm.

The net energy absorbed is 

ΔE=hcλ1-hcλ2=hc10-9(1500-1600)

=6.6×10-34×3×108×100500×600×10-9

=6.6×330×10-19 J

1 eV=1.6×10-19 J

=6.6×330×1.6eV

=4125×10-4 eV

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: Particle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mtext>m</mtext><mtext>A</mtext></msub><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mtext>m</mtext><mn>2</mn></mfrac></math> moving along the <math><mtext>x</mtext></math> -axis with velocity <math><mrow><msub><mtext>v</mtext><mn>0</mn></msub></mrow></math> collides elastically with another particle <math><mtext>B</mtext></math> at rest having mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mtext>m</mtext><mtext>B</mtext></msub><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mtext>m</mtext><mn>3</mn></mfrac></math>. questionIds.solution:
v1=2m/30m2+m3+m2−m3vm2+m3=v5

For particle A,

Initial de-Broglie wavelength

Final de-Broglie wavelength after collision.

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: An alternating emf <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>440</mn><mi>sin</mi><mn>100</mn><mi>π</mi><mi>t</mi></math> is applied to a circuit containing an inductance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt><mi>π</mi></mfrac><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></math>. If an a.c. ammeter is connected in the circuit, its reading will be :
questionIds.solution:
Given that E=440sin100πt, L=2πH

Angular frequency of the source is ω=100π rad s-1.

Now the reactance of the inductor will be,

XL=ωL=100π2π=1002 Ω

Therefore, the peak current I0=E0XL=4401002=2.22 A

AC ammeter reads RMS value therefore reading will be Irms 

Irms=I02=2.2 A

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: Two closed vessels of same volume are joined through a narrow tube and both vessels are filled with air of pressure $90$ kPa and temperature $400$ K. Keeping the temperature of one vessel constant at $400$ K the second vessel temperature is raised to $500$ K. The final pressure in the vessels is _______ kPa.
questionIds.solution:
Let the volume of each vessel be $V$.

Initially, both vessels are at pressure $P_1 = 90$ kPa and temperature $T_1 = 400$ K.

Total number of moles initially is given by:

$n_i = \dfrac{P_1 V}{R T_1} + \dfrac{P_1 V}{R T_1} = \dfrac{2 P_1 V}{R T_1}$

Finally, the temperature of the first vessel is $T_1 = 400$ K and the second vessel is $T_2 = 500$ K. Let the final pressure be $P_f$.

Total number of moles finally is given by:

$n_f = \dfrac{P_f V}{R T_1} + \dfrac{P_f V}{R T_2}$

Since the total number of moles remains constant, $n_i = n_f$:

$\dfrac{2 P_1 V}{R T_1} = \dfrac{P_f V}{R T_1} + \dfrac{P_f V}{R T_2}$

Substituting the given values:

$\dfrac{2 \times 90}{400} = \dfrac{P_f}{400} + \dfrac{P_f}{500}$

$\dfrac{180}{400} = P_f \left( \dfrac{1}{400} + \dfrac{1}{500} \right)$

$\dfrac{180}{400} = P_f \left( \dfrac{5 + 4}{2000} \right)$

$\dfrac{180}{400} = P_f \left( \dfrac{9}{2000} \right)$

$P_f = \dfrac{180 \times 2000}{400 \times 9}$

$P_f = 20 \times 5 = 100$ kPa

Answer: $100$

dm=λ.dv

=λ01+xL.x2dx

I=λ0∫01x2+x3Ldx

=λ0x33+x44L0L

=λ0L33+L34

I=7λ0L312

m=∫0Lλdx=3λ0L2

⇒ I=718mL2

questionIds.subject: Chemistry
questionIds.q:
IUPAC name of the some alkenes are given below. Find out the correct stability order.
A. 2-Methylbut-2-ene
B. cis-But-2-ene
C. 2,3-Dimethylbut-2-ene
D. Prop-1-ene
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
questionIds.solution:
The stability of alkenes is directly proportional to the number of $\alpha$-hydrogen atoms due to hyperconjugation.

Let us count the number of $\alpha$-hydrogen atoms in each given alkene:

A. 2-Methylbut-2-ene: $(CH_3)_2C=CHCH_3$
Number of $\alpha$-hydrogen atoms = $3 + 3 + 3 = 9$

B. cis-But-2-ene: $CH_3CH=CHCH_3$
Number of $\alpha$-hydrogen atoms = $3 + 3 = 6$

C. 2,3-Dimethylbut-2-ene: $(CH_3)_2C=C(CH_3)_2$
Number of $\alpha$-hydrogen atoms = $3 + 3 + 3 + 3 = 12$

D. Prop-1-ene: $CH_3CH=CH_2$
Number of $\alpha$-hydrogen atoms = $3$

Since the stability of an alkene increases with the number of $\alpha$-hydrogen atoms, the correct stability order is C > A > B > D.

Answer: C > A > B > D

questionIds.subject: Chemistry
questionIds.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questionIds.q:
Given below are two statements:
Statement I: The second ionisation enthalpy of Na is larger than the corresponding ionisation enthalpy of Mg.
Statement II: The ionic radius of $\mathrm{O}^{2-}$ is larger than that of $\mathrm{F}^{-}$.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below
options: Statement I is false but Statement II is true
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
questionIds.solution:
Statement I examines second ionization enthalpy:
Na (1s$^2$ 2s$^2$ 2p$^6$ 3s$^1$) loses its first electron to achieve a stable noble gas configuration. The second ionization would break into the 2p core, requiring enormous energy.
Mg (1s$^2$ 2s$^2$ 2p$^6$ 3s$^2$) loses its second electron from the 3s orbital, which is less stable.
Thus, IE$_2$ of Na is much larger than IE$_2$ of Mg.
Statement I is TRUE.
Statement II compares O$^{2-}$ and F$^-$:
both are isoelectronic with 10 electrons.
O$^{2-}$ has 8 protons while F$^-$ has 9 protons.
The lower nuclear charge in O$^{2-}$ results in weaker electrostatic attraction, making the ionic radius of O$^{2-}$ larger than F$^-$.
Statement II is TRUE.

questionIds.subject: Chemistry
questionIds.q:
Consider the reaction :
$$
\mathrm{Cl}_2(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{~S}(\mathrm{aq}) \rightarrow \mathrm{S}(\mathrm{s})+2 \mathrm{H}^{+}(\mathrm{aq})+2 \mathrm{Cl}^{-}(\mathrm{aq})
$$
The rate equation for this reaction is rate $=\mathrm{k}\left[\mathrm{Cl}_2\right]\left[\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{~S}\right]$ Which of these mechanisms is/are consistent with this rate equation?
(A) $\mathrm{Cl}_2+\mathrm{H}_2 \rightarrow \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{Cl}^{-}+\mathrm{Cl}^{+}+\mathrm{HS}^{-} \quad$ (slow)
$$
\mathrm{Cl}^{+}+\mathrm{HS}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{Cl}^{-}+\mathrm{S} \text { (fast) }
$$
(B) $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{~S} \Leftrightarrow \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{HS}^{-} \quad$ (fast equilibrium) $\mathrm{Cl}_2+\mathrm{HS}^{-} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{Cl}^{-}+\mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{S} \quad$ (slow)
questionIds.solution:
Rate equation is to be derived wrt slow
Step $\therefore$ from mechanism (A)
$$
\text { Rate }=\mathrm{k}\left[\mathrm{Cl}_2\right]\left[\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{~S}\right]
$$

questionIds.subject: Chemistry
questionIds.q: The correct order of bond enthalpy (<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>kJ</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>) is:
questionIds.solution:
As bond length increases, bond energy decreases.

Bond Bond energy (kJ mol-1)
C - C 348
Si - Si 297
Ge - Ge 260
Sn - Sn 240

So, bond enthalpy order C - C > Si - Si > Ge - Ge > Sn - Sn

questionIds.subject: Chemistry
questionIds.q:
The enthalpy changes for the following processes are listed below:
$\begin{array}{ll}\mathrm{Cl}_2(\mathrm{~g})=2 \mathrm{Cl}(\mathrm{g}), & 242.3 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \\ \mathrm{I}_2(\mathrm{~g})=2 \mathrm{I}(\mathrm{g}), & 151.0 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \\ \mathrm{ICl}(\mathrm{g})=\mathrm{I}(\mathrm{g})+\mathrm{Cl}(\mathrm{g}), & 211.3 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \\ \mathrm{I}_2(\mathrm{~s})=\mathrm{I}_2(\mathrm{~g}), & 62.76 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\end{array}$
Given that the standard states for iodine and chlorine are $\mathrm{I}_2(\mathrm{~s})$ and $\mathrm{Cl}_2(\mathrm{~g})$, the standard enthalpy of formation for $\mathrm{ICl}(\mathrm{g})$ is
questionIds.solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{I}_2(\mathrm{~s})+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{Cl}_2 \longrightarrow \mathrm{ICl}(\mathrm{g}) \\
& \Delta \mathrm{H}=\left[\frac{1}{2} \Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{b}_2(s) \rightarrow k_2(g)}+\frac{1}{2} \mu_{1-1}+\frac{1}{2} \mu_{\mathrm{cl}-\mathrm{cl}}\right]-\left[\mu_{1-a}\right] \\
& =\left(\frac{1}{2} \times 62.76+\frac{1}{2} \times 151.0+\frac{1}{2} \times 242.3\right)-(211.3) \\
& =228.03-211.3 \\
& \Delta \mathrm{H}=16.73 \\
&
\end{aligned}
$$

questionIds.subject: Chemistry
questionIds.q: The standard electrode potential (<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup><mo>/</mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></math>) for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>Cr</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>Mn</mi></math> & <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Co</mi></math> are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>26</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mo>-</mo><mo> </mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>41</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>57</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>97</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>, respectively. The metal ions which can liberate <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> from a dilute acid are
questionIds.solution:
The E⊝ values for the redox couple (M3+/M2+) shows that Mn2+ and Co2+ ions are the strongest oxidising agents in aqueous solution.

The ions Ti2+, V2+ and Cr2+ are strong reducing agents and will liberate hydrogen from a dilute acid.

So, the Correct answer is V2+ and Cr2+.

questionIds.subject: Chemistry
questionIds.q: The elevation in boiling point for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn></math> molal solution of non-volatile solute <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math>. The depression in freezing point for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn></math> molal solution of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> in the same solvent is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math>. The ratio of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">f</mi></msub></math> i.e., <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi></msub><mo>/</mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">f</mi></msub></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>:</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math> is
questionIds.solution:
We know that

ΔTb=iKbm1ΔTf=iKfm2

And for non-volatile solute i=1.

ΔTbΔTf=Kb×1 Kf×2⇒36=12=KbKf×12

KbKf=11⇒X=1

questionIds.subject: Chemistry
questionIds.q: According to the kinetic theory of gases, in an ideal gas, between two successive collisions a gas molecule travels
questionIds.solution: According to kinetic theory the gas molecules travel in a straight line path but show haphazard motion due to collisions.

questionIds.subject: Chemistry
questionIds.q:
Consider the following data:
$\Delta_{f} \mathrm{H}^{\ominus}$ (methane, g$)=-\mathrm{X} \mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{mol}{ }^{-1}$
Enthalpy of sublimation of graphite $=\mathrm{Y} \mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$
Dissociation enthalpy of $\mathrm{H}_{2}=\mathrm{Z} \mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{mol}{ }^{-1}$
The bond enthalpy of $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{H}$ bond is given by :
questionIds.solution:
For methane formation: $\text{C(g)} + 2\text{H}_2(g) \rightarrow \text{CH}_4(g)$.
Using enthalpy balance: $\Delta_f H° = \text{Bonds broken} - \text{Bonds formed}$.
Breaking: sublimation of C (Y) and 2 H-H bonds (2Z); Forming: 4 C-H bonds.
Energy equation: $-X = Y + 2Z - 4B$ where B is C-H bond enthalpy.
Solving: $4B = X + Y + 2Z$, so $B = \frac{X + Y + 2Z}{4}$.

questionIds.subject: Chemistry
questionIds.q:
The total number of isoelectronic species from the given set is ________.

O2-, F–, Al, Mg2+, Na+, O+, Mg, Al3+, F
questionIds.solution:
Isoelectronic are those which have the same number of electrons. Let us write the number of electrons from the given species.

Species No. of electrons
O–2  10
F- 10
Al 13
Mg2+ 10
Na+ 10
O+ 7
Mg 12
Al3+ 10
F 9

Hence five species among the given are isoelectronic species.

questionIds.subject: Chemistry
questionIds.q:
Total enthalpy change for freezing of 1 mol of water at $10^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to ice at $-10^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is ____
(Given : $\Delta_{\text {fus }} H=x \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$)
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{p}}\left[\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})\right]=\mathrm{y} \mathrm{J} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \mathrm{~K}^{-1} \\ & \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{p}}\left[\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{s})\right]=\mathrm{z} \mathrm{J} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \mathrm{~K}^{-1}\end{aligned}$
questionIds.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \Delta H=1 \times y(0-10)-x \times 1000+1 \times z\left(-10^{\circ}-0^{\circ}\right) \\ & \Delta H=-10(100 x+y+z) \text { Joule. }\end{aligned}$

questionIds.subject: Chemistry
questionIds.q: Consider the following reactions giving major product. Identify the correct reaction.
questionIds.solution:
In reaction (1), Gabriel phthalimide synthesis cannot be used to prepare aniline because aryl halides do not undergo nucleophilic substitution ($S_N2$) with potassium phthalimide due to partial double bond character of the C-X bond.

In reaction (2), the reduction of isocyanide ($R-NC$) with $Sn/HCl$ yields a secondary amine ($R-NHCH_3$), not a primary amine ($R-CH_2NH_2$).

In reaction (3), propanamide reacts with $Br_2$ and $KOH$ (Hoffmann Bromamide Degradation) to give ethanamine. This is a correct reaction.

In reaction (4), the $-NHCO-$ group is attached to two rings. The ring attached to the nitrogen atom is activated (ortho/para directing) due to the lone pair on N, while the ring attached to the carbonyl group is deactivated. Therefore, nitration should occur on the ring attached to the Nitrogen atom at the para position, not on the ring attached to the Carbonyl group.

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q:
Let a line L pass through the origin and be perpendicular to the lines L1:r→=i^-11j^-7k^+λi^+2j^+3k^, λ∈ℝ and
L2:r→=-i^+k^+μ2i^+2j^+k^, μ∈ℝ. If P is the point of intersection of L and L1, and ,Qα,β,γ is the foot of perpendicular from P on L2, then 9α+β+γ is equal to ________.
questionIds.solution:
Given,

A line L pass through the origin and be perpendicular to the lines L1:r→=i^-11j^-7k^+λi^+2j^+3k^, λ∈ℝ and
L2:r→=-i^+k^+μ2i^+2j^+k^, μ∈ℝ

So, direction ratio of line will be,
i^j^k^123221

=i^-4-j^-5+k^-2

=-4i^+5j^-2k^

Hence, the equation of line L which passes through origin will be,

L:r→=σ(-4i^+5j^-2k^)

Now finding the intersection point P we get,

1+λ=-4σ  ....1

-11+2λ=5σ  ....2

-7+3λ=-2σ ....3

So, from equation 1 & 3 we get, 1+λ=-14+6λ⇒λ=3, σ=-1

Hence, point P≡4,-5,2

Now finding the point Q which is foot of perpendicular from point P4,-5,2 on line L2: -i^+k^+μ2i^+2j^+k^ we get,

Now any point on line L2 will be Q2μ-1i^+2μj^+μ+1k^

Now direction ratio of PQ will be -5+2μi^+2μ+5j^+μ-1k^=0

Now using the perpendicular condition we get,

2(-5+2μ)+2(2μ+5)+1(μ-1)=0

⇒μ=19

Hence, α+β+γ=2μ-1+2μ+μ+1=5μ=59

⇒9α+β+γ=5

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: Let y=y(x) be the solution of the differential equation dydx=tanx+ysinxsecx-sinxtanx, x∈0, π2 satisfying the condition yπ4=2. Then, yπ3 is
questionIds.solution:
Given: dydx=tanx+ysinxsecx-sinxtanx

⇒dydx=tanx+ysinx1cosx-sin2xcosx

⇒dydx=tanx+ysinxcos2xcosx

⇒dydx=tanx+ysinxcosx

⇒dydx-2ysin2x=sec2x

⇒IF=e-2∫cosec2xdx

⇒IF=e-logcosec2x-cot2x

⇒IF=1cosec2x-cot2x=cosec2x+cot2x

⇒IF=1sin2x+cos2xsin2x

⇒IF=2cos2x2sinxcosx

⇒IF=cotx

⇒y×cotx=∫cotx×sec2xdx

⇒y×cotx=2∫cosec2xdx

⇒ycotx=logcosec2x-cot2x+c

Now, using yπ4=2 we get,

⇒2cotπ4=logcosecπ2-cotπ2+c

⇒c=2

⇒ycotx=logcosec2x-cot2x+2

Now, finding yπ3 we get,

⇒ycotπ3=logcosec2π3-cot2π3+2

⇒y×13=log23+13+2

⇒y×13=12log3+2

⇒y=32log3+23

⇒y=3log3+2

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: If  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>a</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>b</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math> are unit vectors and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mover><mi>a</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><mover><mi>b</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced></math> is perpendicular to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>7</mn><mover><mi>a</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn><mover><mi>b</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mover><mi>a</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn><mover><mi>b</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced></math> is perpendicular to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>7</mn><mover><mi>a</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mover><mi>b</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo></math> then the angle between <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>a</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>b</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math> (in degrees) is _________.
questionIds.solution:
We have, a→+3b→⊥7a→-5b→

Therefore, a→+3b→·7a→-5b→=0

⇒7a→2-15b→2+16a→·b→=0      …1

and a→-4b→·7a→-2b→=0

⇒7a→2+8b→2-30a→·b→=0     …2

From 1 and 2, we get

2a→cosθ=b→

∴ cosθ=b→2a→

⇒θ=60°.

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.solution:
The given equation is:

$(1 - x^3)^{10} = \sum\limits_{r=0}^{10} a_r x^r (1-x)^{30-2r}$

Using the algebraic identity $1 - x^3 = (1-x)(1+x+x^2)$, the left hand side can be written as:

$(1-x)^{10}(1+x+x^2)^{10} = \sum\limits_{r=0}^{10} a_r x^r (1-x)^{30-2r}$

Dividing both sides by $(1-x)^{30}$:

$\dfrac{(1-x)^{10}(1+x+x^2)^{10}}{(1-x)^{30}} = \sum\limits_{r=0}^{10} a_r \dfrac{x^r}{(1-x)^{2r}}$

$\left( \dfrac{1+x+x^2}{(1-x)^2} \right)^{10} = \sum\limits_{r=0}^{10} a_r \left( \dfrac{x}{(1-x)^2} \right)^r$

Let $y = \dfrac{x}{(1-x)^2}$.

The term inside the bracket on the left hand side can be simplified as:

$\dfrac{1+x+x^2}{(1-x)^2} = \dfrac{1-2x+x^2+3x}{(1-x)^2} = \dfrac{(1-x)^2+3x}{(1-x)^2} = 1 + 3\left(\dfrac{x}{(1-x)^2}\right) = 1 + 3y$

Substituting this into the equation, we get:

$(1 + 3y)^{10} = \sum\limits_{r=0}^{10} a_r y^r$

Using the binomial expansion, $(1 + 3y)^{10} = \sum\limits_{r=0}^{10} {^{10}C_{r}} (3y)^r = \sum\limits_{r=0}^{10} {^{10}C_{r}} 3^r y^r$.

Comparing the coefficients of $y^r$ on both sides, we obtain:

$a_r = {^{10}C_{r}} 3^r$

For $r = 9$ and $r = 10$:

$a_9 = {^{10}C_{9}} 3^9 = 10 \times 3^9$

$a_{10} = {^{10}C_{10}} 3^{10} = 1 \times 3^{10} = 3^{10}$

Therefore, the required value is:

$\dfrac{9a_9}{a_{10}} = \dfrac{9 \times 10 \times 3^9}{3^{10}} = \dfrac{90 \times 3^9}{3 \times 3^9} = \dfrac{90}{3} = 30$

Answer: $30$

questionIds.solution:

$\Rightarrow g^{\prime}(x)=\cos 4 x \quad \Rightarrow g(x)=\frac{\sin 4 x}{4}+k \quad \Rightarrow g(x)=\frac{\sin 4 x}{4}[\because g(0)=0]$
$g(x+\pi)=g(x)+g(\pi)=g(x)-g(\pi)(\because g(\pi)=0)$

questionIds.q: Let the solution curve <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>,</mo><mn>0</mn><mo><</mo><mi>y</mi><mo><</mo><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac></math>, of the differential equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi>log</mi><mi>e</mi></msub><mfenced><mrow><mi>cos</mi><mi>y</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msup><mi>cos</mi><mi>y</mi><mo> </mo><mi>d</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><mi>x</mi><msub><mi>log</mi><mi>e</mi></msub><mfenced><mrow><mi>cos</mi><mi>y</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfenced><mi>sin</mi><mi>y</mi><mo> </mo><mi>d</mi><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>  satisfy <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mfenced><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>3</mn></mfrac></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mrow><mn>2</mn><msub><mi>log</mi><mi>e</mi></msub><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfrac></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mfenced><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>6</mn></mfrac></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mrow><msub><mi>log</mi><mi>e</mi></msub><mi>m</mi><mo>-</mo><msub><mi>log</mi><mi>e</mi></msub><mi>n</mi></mrow></mfrac></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> are coprime, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi><mi>n</mi></math> is equal to
questionIds.solution:

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: If a unit vector <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>a</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover></math> makes angles <math><mfrac><mrow><mi>π</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfrac></math> with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mfrac><mi mathvariant="normal">π</mi><mn>4</mn></mfrac></math> with <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>j</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>θ</mi><mo>∈</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mi>π</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> with <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>k</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>,</mo></math> then a value of <math><mi>θ</mi></math> is:
questionIds.solution:
The unit vector a→ makes angle π3, π4 and θ with i^, j^ & k^ respectively.

Hence, the direction cosines of the vector a→ are cosα=cosπ3=12, cosβ=cosπ4=12 and cosγ=cosθ

For a line having direction cosines as cosα, cosβ, cosγ

⇒cos2α+cos2β+cos2γ=1

⇒14+12+cos2θ=1

⇒cos2θ=1-34=14

⇒cos2θ=±12

⇒θ=π3 or 2π3.

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: Let $f$ be a function such that $f(x)+3 f\left(\frac{24}{x}\right)$ $=4 x, x \neq 0$. Then $f(3)+f(8)$ is equal to
questionIds.solution:
$f(x)+3 f\left(\frac{24}{x}\right)=4 x$
Put $x=3 \quad f(3)+3 f(8)=12$
Put $x=8 \quad f(8)+3 f(3)=32$
Add both $\quad 4(\mathrm{f}(3)+\mathrm{f}(8))=44$
$f(3)+f(8)=11$

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q:
Let for $\mathrm{i}=1,2,3, \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{i}}(\mathrm{x})$ be a polynomial of degree 2 in $x, p^{\prime}{ }_i(x)$ and $p^{\prime \prime}{ }_i(x)$ be the first and second order derivatives of $p_i(x)$ respectively. Let,
$$
\left.\mathrm{A}(\mathrm{x})=\left[\begin{array}{lll}
\mathrm{p}_1(\mathrm{x}) & \mathrm{p}_1^{\prime}(\mathrm{x}) & \mathrm{p}_1^{\prime \prime} \mathrm{x}( \\
\mathrm{p}_2(\mathrm{x}) & \mathrm{p}_2^{\prime}(\mathrm{x}) & \mathrm{p}_2^{\prime \prime}( \\
\mathrm{p}_3(\mathrm{x}) & \mathrm{p}_3^{\prime}(\mathrm{x}) & \mathrm{p}_3^{\prime \prime}(\mathrm{x}
\end{array}\right]\right)
$$
and $\mathrm{B}(\mathrm{x})=[\mathrm{A}(\mathrm{x})]^{\mathrm{T}} \mathrm{A}(\mathrm{x})$. Then determinant of $\mathrm{B}(\mathrm{x})$ :
options: is a polynomial of degree 6 in $\mathrm{x}$.
is a polynomial of degree 3 in $\mathrm{x}$.
is a polynomial of degree 2 in $\mathrm{x}$.
questionIds.solution:
Let $p_1 x=a_1 x^2+b_1 x+c_1$
$$
p_2 x=a_2 x^2+b_2 x+c_2
$$
and $p_3 x=a_3 x^2+b_3 x+c_3$
where $a_1, a_2, a_3, b_1, b_2, b_3, c_1, c_2, c_3$ are real numbers.
$$
\begin{gathered}
\therefore \mathrm{A}(x)=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}
a_1 x^2+b_1 x+c_1 & 2 a_1 x+b_1 & 2 a_1 \\
a_2 x^2+b_2 x+c_2 & 2 a_2 x+b_2 & 2 a_2 \\
a_3 x^2+b_3 x+c_3 & 2 a_3 x+b_3 & 2 a_3
\end{array}\right] \\
=\left[\begin{array}{llll}
a_1 x^2+b_1 x+c_1 & a_2 x^2+b_2 x+c_2 & a_3 x^2+b_3 x+c_3 \\
2 a_1 x+b_1 & 2 a_2 x+b_2 & 2 a_3 x+b_2 \\
2 a_1 & 2 a_2 & 2 a_3
\end{array}\right] \\
\times\left[\begin{array}{ccc}
a_1 x^2+b_1 x+c_1 & 2 a_1 x+b_1 & 2 a_1 \\
a_2 x^2+b_2 x+c_2 & 2 a_2 x+b_2 & 2 a_2 \\
a_3 x^2+b_3 x+c_3 & 2 a_3 x+b_3 & 2 a_3
\end{array}\right]
\end{gathered}
$$
It is clear from the above multiplication, the degree of determinant of $\mathrm{B}(x)$ can not be less than 4 .

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: For $\lambda>0$, let $\theta$ be the angle between the vectors $\vec{a}=\hat{i}+\lambda \hat{j}-3 \hat{k}$ and $\vec{b}=3 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}$. If the vectors $\vec{a}+\vec{b}$ and $\vec{a}-\vec{b}$ are mutually perpendicular, then the value of (14 cos $\theta)^2$ is equal to
questionIds.solution: $\begin{aligned} & (\vec{a}+\vec{b}) \cdot(\vec{a}-\vec{b})=0, \lambda>0 \\ & |\vec{a}|^2-|\vec{b}|^2=0 \rightarrow 1+\lambda^2+9=9+1+4 \\ & \therefore \lambda=2, \cos \theta=\frac{\vec{a}-\vec{b}}{|\vec{a}| \cdot|\vec{b}|}=\frac{3-\lambda-6}{\sqrt{14} \cdot \sqrt{14}} \\ & 14 \cos \theta=3-8=-5 \\ & \therefore(14 \cos \theta)^2=25\end{aligned}$

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced open="{" close="}"><mrow><mn>11</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>12</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>13</mn><mo>,</mo><mo>…</mo><mo>.</mo><mo>,</mo><mn>40</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>41</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Y</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced open="{" close="}"><mrow><mn>61</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>62</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>63</mn><mo>,</mo><mo>.</mo><mo>.</mo><mo>.</mo><mo>,</mo><mn>90</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>91</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> be the two sets of observations. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>¯</mo></mover></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi><mo>¯</mo></mover></math> are their respective means and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>σ</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> is the variance of all the observations in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi><mo>∪</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Y</mi></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="|" close="|"><mrow><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>¯</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi><mo>¯</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><msup><mi>σ</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced></math> is equal to ________
questionIds.solution:
We have,

X=11,12,13,….,40,41

⇒nX=31

Y=61,62,63,...,90,91

⇒nY=31

So,

x¯=∑i=1141i31=13141×422-10×112=26

y¯=∑i=6191i31=13191×922-60×612=73

Combined mean,

 μ=31×26+31×7631+31

⇒μ=26+762=51

Also,

σ2=162×∑i=131xi-μ2+∑i=131yi-μ2

Since, xi∈X are in A.P. with 31 elements  common difference 1 , same is yi∈y, when written in increasing order.

∴∑i=131xi-μ2=∑i=131yi-μ2=102+112+….+402

=102+112+….+402

=40×41×816-9×10×196=21855

Hence, σ2=2185562+2185562=705

∴x¯+y¯-σ2=|26+76-705|=603

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>)</mo></math> be the solution of the differential equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>3</mn><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced><mi>y</mi><mi>d</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mfenced><mrow><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced><mi>d</mi><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>(</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>)</mo><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math>. Then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="|" close="|"><mrow><mo>(</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>(</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>)</mo><msup><mo>)</mo><mn>3</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>12</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>(</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>)</mo></mrow></mfenced></math> is equal to :
questionIds.solution:
Given,

3y2-5x2ydx+2xx2-y2dy=0

⇒dydx=y5x2-3y22xx2-y2

This is homogeneous differential equation

Let y=tx⇒dydx=t+xdtdx

t+xdtdx=(tx)5x2-3t2x22xx2-t2x2=t5-3t221-t2

⇒xdtdx=5t-3t32-2t2-t=3t-t32-2t2

⇒21-t23t-t3dt=1xdx

Taking integration on both sides we get,

∫21-t23t-t3dt=∫1xdx......1

Now let 3t-t3=z⇒31-t2dt=dz

⇒1-t2dt=13dz

Substitute in equation 1 we get,

23∫dzz=∫1xdx

⇒23ln(z)=lnx+lnc

⇒z23=cx⇒z=c32x32

⇒3t-t3=c32x32

Now put t=yx

⇒3yx-y3x3=c32x32

Given y(1)=1 when x=1,y=1

 ⇒c32=2

So, equation becomes 3yx-y3x3=2x32

⇒3x2y-y3=2x92......2

Put x=2 in equation 2,

12y(2)-y(2)3=2(2)92=322

∴y(2)3-12y(2)=322

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: The probability that a randomly selected <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>-</mo></math>digit number belongs to the set <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="" open="{"><mrow><mi>n</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi>N</mi><mo>:</mo><mfenced><mrow><msup><mn>2</mn><mi>n</mi></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfenced></math> is a multiple of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>}</mo></math> is equal to
questionIds.solution:
Total two-digit numbers is 90

So, total number of cases =C190=90

Now, 2n-2=3-1n-2

=C0n3n-C1n·3n-1+…+-1n-1·Cn-1n3+-1n·Cnn-2

=33n-1-n3n-2+…+-1n-1·n+-1n-2

Here, the term -1n-2 should be multiple of 3.

So, 2n-2 is multiple of 3 only when n is odd.

There are total 45 odd two-digit numbers.

Hence, the required probability =C14590=4590=12

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: The product of all the rational roots of the equation $\left(x^2-9 x+11\right)^2-(x-4)(x-5)=3$, is equal to
questionIds.solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \left(x^2-9 x+11\right)^2-(x-4)(x-5)=3 \\
& \left(x^2-9 x+11\right)^2-\left(x^2-9 x+20\right)=3
\end{aligned}$
Let $x^2-9 x+11=t$
$\begin{aligned}
& t^2-(t+9)=3 \\
& \Rightarrow t^2-t-12=0 \\
& \Rightarrow t^2-4 t+3 t-12=0 \\
& \Rightarrow t(t-4)+3(t-4)=0 \\
& \Rightarrow t=4 \text { or }-3 \\
& x^2-9 x+11=4 \\
& x^2-9 x+7=0
\end{aligned}$
Here, we will get irrational roots
$\begin{aligned}
& x^2-9 x+11=-3 \\
& x^2-9 x+14=0 \\
& x^2-7 x-2 x+14=0 \\
& \Rightarrow x=7,2 \\
& \Rightarrow \text { Product of all rational roots }=14
\end{aligned}$

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo> </mo></math>is a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>×</mo><mn>3</mn></math> non-singular matrix such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><msup><mi>A</mi><mo>'</mo></msup><mo>=</mo><msup><mi>A</mi><mo>'</mo></msup><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi><mo>=</mo><msup><mi>A</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mi>A</mi><mo>'</mo></msup><mi>,</mi></math> then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi><msup><mi>B</mi><mo>'</mo></msup></math> equals, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>X</mi><mo>'</mo></msup></math> denotes the transpose of the matrix <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math>.
questionIds.solution:
A'=AT

AA'=A'A

B=A-1A'

B'=A-1A''=A''·A-1'=AA-1'

BB'=A-1A'·AA-1'

       =A-1·A·A'·A-1'     ∵  AA'=A'A

       =I·A'·A-1'

       =I·A-1A'           ∵  CD'=D'C'

       =I·I'

       =I2                                      ∵  I=I'

       =I

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: The number of positive integers <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>k</mi></math> such that the constant term in the binomial expansion of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>3</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mfrac><mn>3</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mi>k</mi></msup></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mn>12</mn></msup><mo>,</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>≠</mo><mn>0</mn></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>2</mn><mn>8</mn></msup><mo>·</mo><mi>l</mi></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>l</mi></math> is an odd integer, is ______.
questionIds.solution:
For 2x3+3xk12

Tr+1=Cr122x312-r·3xkr=Cr12·212-r·3r·x36-3r-kr

For the term to be independent of x,

36-3r-kr=0 ⇒r=36k+3 

Here k can be 0,1,3,6,9 for r to be a whole number.

But for Cr12·212-r·3r to be in the form of 28·l

Only k=3, 6 satisfies the above relation.

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi mathvariant="bold-italic">N</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="]" open="["><mi>x</mi></mfenced></math> denote the greatest integer less than or equal to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>.</mo></math> If the sum of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>n</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> terms of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mmultiscripts><mi>C</mi><mn>0</mn><none></none><mprescripts></mprescripts><none></none><mi>n</mi></mmultiscripts><mo>,</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>·</mo><mmultiscripts><mi>C</mi><mn>1</mn><none></none><mprescripts></mprescripts><none></none><mi>n</mi></mmultiscripts><mo>,</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>·</mo><mmultiscripts><mi>C</mi><mn>2</mn><none></none><mprescripts></mprescripts><none></none><mi>n</mi></mmultiscripts><mo>,</mo><mn>7</mn><mo>·</mo><mmultiscripts><mi>C</mi><mn>3</mn><none></none><mprescripts></mprescripts><none></none><mi>n</mi></mmultiscripts><mo>,</mo><mo>…</mo></math> is equal to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>2</mn><mn>100</mn></msup><mo>·</mo><mn>101</mn><mo>,</mo></math> then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mfenced close="]" open="["><mfrac><mrow><mi>n</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow><mn>2</mn></mfrac></mfenced></math> is equal to
questionIds.solution:
Let S=C0n+3C1n+5.C2n+…+2n+1·Cnn

Now, Tr=2r+1nCr

Therefore, S=ΣTr

S=Σ2r+1nCr

=Σ2rnCr+ΣnCr

⇒S=2n·2n-1+2n=2nn+1

⇒2nn+1=2100.101

⇒n=100

Now, 2n-12=2992=98.

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>w</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>I</mi><mi>m</mi><mo> </mo><mi>w</mi><mo>≠0</mo></mrow></mfenced></math> be a complex number. Then, the set of all complex numbers <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>z</mi></math> satisfying the equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>w</mi><mo>-</mo><mover><mi>w</mi><mo>¯</mo></mover><mi>z</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>k</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>-</mo><mi>z</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>, for some real number <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo mathvariant="italic">k</mo></math>, is
questionIds.solution:
w∈C,  Im w≠0 ⇒w=a+ib:b≠0 (suppose)

w-w¯ z=k1-z

If z=x+iy

⇒a+ib-a-ibx+iy=k{1-x+iy}

⇒a+ib-ax+iay-ibx+by=k-kx-iky

Equating real and imaginary parts

a-ax-by=k-kx    …(1)

and b-ay+bx=-ky     …2

From 1 k-ax-1=by

 2 k-ay=b(x+1)
Dividing the two equations, we get, 

x-1y=yx+1

⇒x2-1=y2

⇒x2+y2=1

⇒z=1

z≠1+i0 (∴ if z=1 then from original equation   w=w ⇒a+ib=a-ib⇒b=0)

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: For $\mathrm{n} \geq 2$, let $S_n$ denote the set of all subsets of $\{1,2 \ldots . . ., n\}$ with no two consecutive numbers. For example $\{1,3,5\} \in \mathrm{S}_6$, but $\{1,2,4\} \notin \mathrm{S}_6$. Then $n\left(\mathrm{~S}_5\right)$ is equal to ________
questionIds.solution:
$\mathrm{A}=\{1,2,3,4,5 \ldots . . . \mathrm{n}\}$
No. of subsets having $r$ elements such that no two are consecutive is $={ }^{n-r+1} C_r$
for $\mathrm{n}=5$, no. of ways $={ }^{6 \cdot \mathrm{r}} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{r}}$
Subsets having no element $=1$
Subsets having exactly 1 element $={ }^5 \mathrm{C}_1=5$
Subsets having exactly 2 element $={ }^4 \mathrm{C}_2=6$
Subsets having exactly 3 element $={ }^3 \mathrm{C}_3=1$
$\Rightarrow 5+6+1+1=13$

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: In a geometric progression consisting of positive terms, each term equals the sum of the next two terms. Then the common ratio of this progression equals
questionIds.solution:
Given $a r^{n-1}=a r^n+a r^{n+1}$
$\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow 1=r+r^2 \\
& \therefore r=\frac{\sqrt{5}-1}{2} .
\end{aligned}$

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: If $\alpha \neq \beta$ but $\alpha^2=5 \alpha-3$ and $\beta^2=5 \beta-3$ then the equation having $\alpha / \beta$ and $\beta / \alpha$ as its roots is
questionIds.solution:
We have $\alpha^2=5 \alpha-3$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow \alpha^2-5 \alpha+3=0 \Rightarrow \alpha=\frac{5 \pm \sqrt{13}}{2} \text {. Similarly, } \beta^2=5 \beta-3 \Rightarrow \alpha=\frac{5 \pm \sqrt{13}}{2} \\
& \therefore \alpha=\frac{5+\sqrt{13}}{2} \text { and } \beta=\frac{5-\sqrt{13}}{2} \text { or vice - versa } \\
& \alpha^2+\beta^2=\frac{50+26}{4}=19 \& \alpha \beta=\frac{1}{4}(25-13)=3
\end{aligned}
$$
Thus, the equation having $\frac{\alpha}{\beta} \& \frac{\beta}{\alpha}$ as its roots is
$$
x^2-x\left(\frac{\alpha}{\beta}+\frac{\beta}{\alpha}\right)+\frac{\alpha \beta}{\alpha \beta}=0 \Rightarrow x^2-x\left(\frac{\alpha^2+\beta^2}{\alpha \beta}\right)+1=0 \text { or } 3 x^2-19 x+1=0
$$

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: From 6 different novels and 3 different dictionaries, 4 novels and 1 dictionary are to be selected and arranged in a row on the shelf so that the dictionary is always in the middle. Then the number of such arrangements is
questionIds.solution:
4 novels can be selected from 6 novels in ${ }^6 \mathrm{C}_4$ ways. 1 dictionary can be selected from 3 dictionaries in ${ }^3 \mathrm{C}_1$ ways. As the dictionary selected is fixed in the middle, the remaining 4 novels can be arranged in 4 ! ways.
$\therefore$ The required number of ways of arrangement $={ }^6 \mathrm{C}_4 \times{ }^3 \mathrm{C}_1 \times 4 !=1080$

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q:
If the equation of the line passing through the point $\left(0,-\frac{1}{2}, 0\right)$ and perpendicular to the lines $\vec{r}=\lambda(\hat{i}+a \hat{j}+b \hat{k})$ and
$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}=(\hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}-6 \hat{\mathrm{k}})+\mu(-b \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\mathrm{a} \hat{\mathrm{j}}+5 \hat{\mathrm{k}})$
is $\frac{\mathrm{x}-1}{-2}=\frac{\mathrm{y}+4}{\mathrm{~d}}=\frac{\mathrm{z}-\mathrm{c}}{-4}$, then $\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}+\mathrm{c}+\mathrm{d}$ is equal to :
questionIds.solution:
Line is $\perp^{\mathrm{r}}$ to 2 line $\Rightarrow$ line will be parallel to
$(i+a \hat{j}+b \hat{k}) \times(-b \hat{i}+a \hat{j}+5 \hat{k})$
Parallel vector along the required line is
$\hat{\mathrm{i}}(5 \mathrm{a}-\mathrm{ab})-\hat{\mathrm{j}}\left(\mathrm{~b}^2+5\right)+\hat{\mathrm{k}}(\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{ab})$
Dr's of required line $\alpha(5 a-a b),-\left(b^2+5\right),(a+a b)$
Also Dr's of required line $\alpha-2, \mathrm{~d},-4$

Also point $\left(0, \frac{-1}{2}, 0\right)$ will lie on $\frac{\mathrm{x}-1}{-2}=\frac{\mathrm{y}+4}{\mathrm{~d}}=\frac{\mathrm{z}-\mathrm{c}}{-4}$
$\frac{0-1}{-2}=\frac{\frac{-1}{2}+4}{d}=\frac{0-c}{-4} \Rightarrow d=7, c=2$
$\begin{aligned}
&\text { From (1) } \frac{5 \mathrm{a}-\mathrm{ab}}{-2}=\frac{-\mathrm{b}^2-5}{7}=\frac{\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{ab}}{-4}\\ &\frac{5 \mathrm{a}-\mathrm{ab}}{-2}=\frac{\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{ab}}{-4} ; \frac{-\mathrm{b}^2-5}{7}=\frac{\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{ab}}{-4}
\end{aligned}$
\begin{array}{c|c}-20 a+4 a b=-2 a-2 a b & 4 b^2+20=70+7 a b \\18 a=6 a b & 36+20=70+21 a \\b=3 & 56=28 a \Rightarrow a=2\end{array}
$a+b+c+d=2+3+2+7=14$

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: The area of the region bounded by the curves $y=|x-1|$ and $y=3-|x|$ is
questionIds.solution: $A=\int_{-1}^0\{(3+x)-(-x+1)\} d x+\int_0^1\{(3-x)-(-x+1)\} d x+\int_1^2\{(3-x)-(-x-1)\} d x$ $=\int_{-1}^0(2+2 x) d x+\int_0^1 2 d x+\int_1^2(4-2 x) d x$ $=\left[2 x-x^2\right]_{-1}^0+[2 x]_0^1+\left[4 x-x^2\right]_1^2$ $=0-(-2+1)+(2-0)+(8-4)-(4-1)$ $=1+2+4-3=4$ sq. units

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: The value of the limit <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><munder><mi>lim</mi><mrow><mi>θ</mi><mo>→</mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></munder><mfrac><mrow><mi>tan</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>π</mi><msup><mi>cos</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mi>θ</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow><mrow><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>π</mi><msup><mi>sin</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mi>θ</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfrac></math> is equal to :
questionIds.solution:
We have to find l=limθ→0tanπcos2θsin2πsin2θ

Using sin2θ+cos2θ=1, we get

⇒l=limθ→0tanπ1-sin2θsin2πsin2θ

⇒l=limθ→0tanπ-πsin2θsin2πsin2θ

Using tanπ-θ=-tanθ, we get

⇒l=limθ→0-tanπsin2θsin2πsin2θ

⇒l=limθ→0-tanπsin2θπsin2θ2πsin2θsin2πsin2θ×12

Using, limx→0sinxx=1 & limx→0tanxx=1, we get

l=-12.
